metadata.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 1
Introduction
What is FDO88?
FDOS8S8 is the Form Definition Opcode (FDO) programming language for the
Macintosh®; Apple®Il, and Tandy® platforms. This language controls the
appearance and functionality of online service windows (forms).
About This Manual
This manual lists, describes, and provides examples of all commands that
make up the FDOS88 language. It defines the parameters used by the FDO
commands and provides sample FDO data streams. This manual also
illustrates the Macintosh window types that can be specified by FDO
commands.
Who Should Use This Manual
This manual is intended for use by experienced Macintosh programmers who
want to develop additional functionality for the Macintosh platform by creating
or modifying forms.
Chapter Overview
The contents of this manual are as follows:
Chapter 1, "Introduction," defines the FDO88 language and discusses the
content of this manual.
Chapter 2, "FDO88 Description," describes the FDO88 language. It contains a
technical overview of FDO88 and provides descriptions, syntax, examples, and
platforms for each FDO command.

Chapter 1: Introduction January 1994
Appendix A, “Glossary,” contains definitions of some basic terms and
concepts used throughout this manual.
Appendix B, “Sample FDO Streams,” presents five sample FDO data streams
generated by the form_edit tool and their resulting screen displays.
Appendix C, “Reference Tables,” provides tables that define the parameters
used by the FDO commands.
Appendix D, “FDO Quick Reference Lists,” lists all FDO commands in
alphabetical and numerical order. The lists contain the FDO command names
and the hexadecimal and decimal values for each opcode. Subopcode
hexadecimal and decimal values are provided where applicable.
Appendix E, “Menu Operation (MOP) Codes,” explains the MOP codes used
in the dispatch argument of the fdo$dispch, fdo$ImdDispatch,
fdo$SMenultem, fdo$SNotifDispatch, and fdo$ResetDispch commands.
Appendix F, “Macintosh Window Types,” provides illustrations of Macintosh
window types specified with the FDO command parameter <window_type>.

sections.content:
Appendix A, "Glossary," contains definitions of some basic terms and concepts used throughout this manual.

Appendix B, "Sample FDO Streams," presents five sample FDO data streams generated by the form_edit tool and their resulting screen displays.

Appendix C, "Reference Tables," provides tables that define the parameters used by the FDO commands.

Appendix D, "FDO Quick Reference Lists," lists all FDO commands in alphabetical and numerical order. The lists contain the FDO command names and the hexadecimal and decimal values for each opcode. Subopcode hexadecimal and decimal values are provided where applicable.

Appendix E, "Menu Operation (MOP) Codes," explains the MOP codes used in the dispatch argument of the fdo$dispch, fdo$ImdDispatch, fdo$SMenultem, fdo$SNotifDispatch, and fdo$ResetDispch commands.

Appendix F, "Macintosh Window Types," provides illustrations of Macintosh window types specified with the FDO command parameter <window_type>.

metadata.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 2
FDO88 Description
Chapter 2 provides a description of the FDO88 language for the Macintosh,
Apple 11, and Tandy platforms. It contains a technical overview of FDO88 and
provides definitions for all FDO commands.
Technical Overview
This section discusses the following subjects:
* FDO command basics
* Modified FDO arguments
* Byte-count FDO commands
* Extended FDO commands
* How FDO streams are sent
FDO Command Basics
Each FDO command has an alphabetic name and a numeric code (opcode).
You use the alphabetic name when building forms; however, you must know
the opcode if you are trying to interpret a raw data stream.
A few FDO commands have a sub-opcode. These exceptions are discussed in
the “Extended FDO Commands” section below.
In this chapter, all FDO commands are presented using their alphabetic name.
The alphabetic names of FDO commands are not case-sensitive; upper case
characters are used only for ease of interpretation when a command name is
made up of two or more words or parts of words.
America Online Confidential 2-1 © America Online, inc. 1993-94

pages.page_footer: America Online Confidential 2-1 © America Online, inc. 1993-94

sections.content: This section discusses the following subjects:
sections.heading: Technical Overview

sections.content: Each FDO command has an alphabetic name and a numeric code (opcode). You use the alphabetic name when building forms; however, you must know the opcode if you are trying to interpret a raw data stream. A few FDO commands have a sub-opcode. These exceptions are discussed in the "Extended FDO Commands" section below. In this chapter, all FDO commands are presented using their alphabetic name. The alphabetic names of FDO commands are not case-sensitive; upper case characters are used only for ease of interpretation when a command name is made up of two or more words or parts of words.
sections.heading: FDO Command Basics

metadata.ocr_text:
Chapter 2: FDO88 Description January 1994
Several FDO commands cannot stand alone. For example, a form does not
compile successfully if the fdo$Text command is present without either the
fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command. Another example is the fdo$SetStyle
command, which does not function if the fdo$Text command is not present.
An FDO command is followed by its command line arguments (parameters).
These parameters provide options in the use of the command. For example,
the fdo$Field command has a <type> parameter that requires specification of
the field type. You can make the field type a button, a text field, or any of the
other available field types.
Modified FDO Arguments
Two types of FDO arguments are modified for transmission in an FDO data
stream — flipped integer arguments and zero-terminated text arguments.
Flipped integers are two-byte integer arguments that have their low (or second)
byte transmitted first, followed by their high (or first) byte. A normal two-byte
integer has its high byte and then low byte transmitted.
Zero-terminated text arguments are variable-length character string arguments
with a 0 appended to them. Software interpreting an FDO data stream expects
zero-terminated text arguments to end with a 0. The modified arguments may
not contain a 0. Both flipped integer and zero-terminated text arguments are
noted as such in the “FDO Command Definitions™ section below.
Byte-Count FDO Commands
Extended
Byte-count FDO commands are made by having their high-bits set. The high-
bit is the eighth bit in an eight-bit byte. The eight bits are assigned positions 0
through seven. In binary notation, the high-bit, if set, adds 27 (or 128) to the
value of the byte. High bits are set by adding 128 to the decimal value or $80 to
the hexadecimal value of an FDO command.
Byte-count FDO commands have byte count arguments which indicate the total
length of the command's parameters; base FDO commands do not. The byte-
count argument is rarely seen except in cases where the raw data stream is
being examined by a developer, and does not need to be entered when building
a form. When you look at a raw data stream, the byte-count parameter is the
second value in the FDO command statement. It appears just after the FDO
command's opcode.
FDO Commands
As the FDO language grew, available opcodes were being depleted. Extended
FDO commands were created to enable a large number of new commands to

Several FDO commands cannot stand alone. For example, a form does not compile successfully if the fdo$Text command is present without either the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command. Another example is the fdo$SetStyle command, which does not function if the fdo$Text command is not present.

An FDO command is followed by its command line arguments (parameters). These parameters provide options in the use of the command. For example, the fdo$Field command has a <type> parameter that requires specification of the field type. You can make the field type a button, a text field, or any of the other available field types.

Two types of FDO arguments are modified for transmission in an FDO data stream — flipped integer arguments and zero-terminated text arguments.

Flipped integers are two-byte integer arguments that have their low (or second) byte transmitted first, followed by their high (or first) byte. A normal two-byte integer has its high byte and then low byte transmitted.

Zero-terminated text arguments are variable-length character string arguments with a 0 appended to them. Software interpreting an FDO data stream expects zero-terminated text arguments to end with a 0. The modified arguments may not contain a 0. Both flipped integer and zero-terminated text arguments are noted as such in the "FDO Command Definitions™ section below.
sections.heading: Modified FDO Arguments

sections.content:
Extended

Byte-count FDO commands are made by having their high-bits set. The high-bit is the eighth bit in an eight-bit byte. The eight bits are assigned positions 0 through seven. In binary notation, the high-bit, if set, adds 27 (or 128) to the value of the byte. High bits are set by adding 128 to the decimal value or $80 to the hexadecimal value of an FDO command.

Byte-count FDO commands have byte count arguments which indicate the total length of the command's parameters; base FDO commands do not. The byte-count argument is rarely seen except in cases where the raw data stream is being examined by a developer, and does not need to be entered when building a form. When you look at a raw data stream, the byte-count parameter is the second value in the FDO command statement. It appears just after the FDO command's opcode.
sections.heading: Byte-Count FDO Commands

sections.content: As the FDO language grew, available opcodes were being depleted. Extended FDO commands were created to enable a large number of new commands to
sections.heading: FDO Commands

metadata.ocr_text:
FDO88 Manual
fit into the 128 byte limit for opcodes. These commands comprise a whole
new class of FDO commands. The format of these commands includes the
use of an FDO command name, which is not unique, and a sub-opcode, which
is unique, in the command syntax.
Since these commands have their high bit set, the opcodes for them fall in the
range of 248 (for the first group of extended commands) through 256 (for the
last group of extended commands). This means that each FDO command in
the first group of extended FDO commands has an opcode of 248 followed by
its own unique sub-opcode, the second group of FDO commands has an
opcode of 249 followed by its own unique sub-opcode, and so on. You can to
assign up to 256 extended FDO commands for each group.
How FDO Streams are Sent
FDO streams can be stored in the local client database or stored and sent from
the host computer. FDO streams sent from the host computer are divided into
data packets. Each data packet is marked with a token which is a two byte
character indicating what kind of data the packet contains.
Packets containing FDO streams are sent with a packet identifier called an fD
token that signifies that the packet contains form definition information. The
fD token is placed in the first two bytes of the data portion of the packet.
FDO Command Definitions
This section provides definitions of all FDO88 commands in alphabetical order.
The FDO command definitions list the associated opcodes along with
descriptions, syntax, examples, and platforms for each command. Sub-opcodes
are also provided, where applicable. The hexadecimal value for each opcode and
sub-opcode is shown in parentheses after the decimal value.
Conventions
The following conventions are used in the FDO command definitions:
fdo$command Bold text beginning with "fdo$" denotes the FDO
command's alphabetic name.
<req_parm> Angle brackets denote required FDO command
parameters.
(<parm1> <parm2>) Parentheses denote a group of corollary parameters.
Each parameter must occur sequentially with all other
parameters shown within the parentheses.

This means that each FDO command in the first group of extended FDO commands has an opcode of 248 followed by its own unique sub-opcode, the second group of FDO commands has an opcode of 249 followed by its own unique sub-opcode, and so on. You can to assign up to 256 extended FDO commands for each group.

metadata.ocr_text:
Chapter 2: FDOB88 Description January 1994
Examples
Examples in the FDO command definitions reflect how the FDO command
line appears in its decompiled format. The decompiled format shows how the
command stream looks before a new form has been compiled, or after an
existing form is selected from the local client database or the host computer and
decompiled. If you have compiled an FDO data stream and are viewing its raw
format, it will not appear like the examples provided in this chapter. Only the
numeric values of FDO commands exist in a raw stream.
In the FDO command examples, some parameter values are shown in
hexadecimal form while others are shown in decimal form. When building a
form you can use either the decimal or hexadecimal form for any parameter
value. However, once the form is compiled, the parameter values you entered
when the form was created may be converted from decimal to hexadecimal or
vice versa by the compiler the next time you decompile the form.
All parameters are converted to hexadecimal values as the form is compiled. In
its compiled state, the FDO data stream is ready to be interpreted by the FDO
engine.
America Online Confidential 2-4 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94

pages.page_footer: America Online Confidential 2-4 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94

Examples in the FDO command definitions reflect how the FDO command line appears in its decompiled format. The decompiled format shows how the command stream looks before a new form has been compiled, or after an existing form is selected from the local client database or the host computer and decompiled. If you have compiled an FDO data stream and are viewing its raw format, it will not appear like the examples provided in this chapter. Only the numeric values of FDO commands exist in a raw stream.

In the FDO command examples, some parameter values are shown in hexadecimal form while others are shown in decimal form. When building a form you can use either the decimal or hexadecimal form for any parameter value. However, once the form is compiled, the parameter values you entered when the form was created may be converted from decimal to hexadecimal or vice versa by the compiler the next time you decompile the form.

All parameters are converted to hexadecimal values as the form is compiled. In its compiled state, the FDO data stream is ready to be interpreted by the FDO engine.

commands.description: fdo$248_Extended $23 (EndListItem) ends the definition of a list box item. fdo$EndDef also ends the definition of the current list box item. fdo$StartListItem ends the definition of a list box item before it starts a new list box item.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

metadata.ocr_text:
FDOB88 Manual
fdo$248 Extended
(EndListItem)
Opcode: 248 ($F8)
Sub-Opcode: 35 ($23)
Description
fdo$248_Extended $23 (EndListItem) ends the definition of a list box item.
fdo$EndDef also ends the definition of the current list box item.
fdo$StartListItem ends the definition of a list box item before it starts a new
list box item.
Syntax
fdo$248_Extended $23 (no parameters)
Example
fdo$248_Extended $23
Platforms
Macintosh

metadata.ocr_text:
Chapter 2: FDO88 Description January 1994
fdo$248 Extended
(FieldLogNum)
Opcode: 248 ($F8)
Sub-Opcode: 17 ($11)
Description
fdo$248_Extended $11 (FieldLogNum) moves text data from a field to the
System log, the Debugging log, the Chat log, the Instant message log, or the
Mail log.
Syntax
fdo$248_Extended $11<field_no> <log_no>
<field_no> 1-byte parameter that specifies the number of the field
whose log file receives the text data.
<log_no> 2-byte integer parameter that specifies the log file to
which the field's contents are written. Values are as
follows:
0 Do not log this field's contents
1 System log
2 Debugging log
3 Chat log
4 Instant message log
5 Mail log
Example
fdo$248_Extended $11 04
This example logs the contents of field 0 (the current field) to the instant
message log (4).
Platforms
Macintosh
America Online Confidential 2-6 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94

commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

commands.description:
fdo$248_Extended $0A (NoAutoShowWindow) prevents the client software from automatically deciding when to show a window. This command is rarely used and is intended as a alternate solution in case the client software's built-in algorithm breaks down and the window is drawn before all of its fields are defined.

Issue this command immediately after you issue the command that creates the window (e.g., fdo$Window16) and before an fdo$SetWindowTag command.

If you issue an fdo$248_Extended $0A (NoAutoShowWindow) command you must also issue an fdo$248_Extended $0B (ResizeWindowNow) command when it is appropriate to show the window. Otherwise, the window is not drawn until you issue the fdo$enddef command. For more information, see the fdo$248_Extended $0B (ResizeWindowNow) definition.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

commands.description:
fdo$248_Extended $08 (RememberSizeID) assigns a resize ID to the current window. This resize ID lets a member save a window's size by selecting Remember Window Size from the Window menu. The next time a window with that resize ID is created, it is shown at the remembered size.
Assign resize IDs centrally to avoid conflict. Issue the fdo$248_Extended $08 (RememberSizeID) command immediately after you issue the command that creates the window (e.g., fdo$Window16).
Windows that do not contain an fdo$248_Extended $08 (RememberSizelD) command, but do have a cluster assigned using fdo$SetWindowTag, will also have Remember Window Size enabled. These windows use the cluster and ID as an alternate form of the remember size ID function. For windows that have both a cluster and a resize ID, the resize ID takes precedence.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

parameters.description: parameter that defines the ID used to remember the window's size.
parameters.name: <resize_ID>

commands.description: Parameter values defining resizing rules for field positioning. Value $05 moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window (e.g., if a field is 10 pixels left of center in a 100% larger window, it remains 10 pixels left). Value $06 moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field. Value $07 combines value $05 with proportional resizing based on window size changes (e.g., X=25 becomes X=50 when window widens from 100 to 200). Value $08 combines value $06 with proportional resizing based on the major field's size changes.
commands.example_explanation: This example creates a button in the current field (00) that maintains the same horizontal distance from the center of the window ($04) when the member resizes the window. The vertical rule is not changed ($00).
commands.name: fdo$FieldRuleX and fdo$FieldRuleY Parameter Values

parameters.description: Specifies the new resizing rule to be used for sizing and positioning the target field in the X (horizontal) direction.
parameters.name: <FieldRuleX>

values.description: Moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window.
Moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field.
Moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window (same as value $05). In addition, the field is resized in proportion to the change in size of the window.
Moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field (same as value $06). In addition, the field is resized in proportion to the change in size of the major field.

parameters.description: Specifies the new resizing rule to be used for sizing and positioning the target field in the Y (vertical) direction. Values are the same as those shown for <FieldRuleX> above.

values.description: Moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window.
Moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field.
Moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window (same as value $05). In addition, the field is resized in proportion to the change in size of the window.
Moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field (same as value $06). In addition, the field is resized in proportion to the change in size of the major field.

commands.description: fdo$248_Extended $0B (ResizeWindowNow) tells the client software when it is safe to show a window. If you issue an fdo$248_Extended $08 (ResizeWindowNow) command, you must also issue an fdo$248_Extended S0A (NoAutoShowWindow) command when it is appropriate to show the window. Otherwise, the window is not drawn until you issue the fdo$enddef command. For more information, see the fdo$248_Extended $0A (NoAutoShowWindow) definition. Issue the fdo$248_Extended $0B (ResizeWindowNow) command after the last field is defined and before content is installed into the form. That way, the client software has all the information it needs to show the window's fields in the correct places, and the member can see the window's contents as they are defined.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

commands.description:
fdo$248_Extended $12 (SetClosePriority) sets a priority value for the current window. This value is used when the client software needs to close open window(s) because of a low memory condition.
When the client software needs more memory, the system closes windows in the order of priority value, starting at 0, then 1, then 2, etc. (regardless of whether they are positive or negative) until the memory problem is alleviated.
If a member refuses to close a window after receiving a request to do so, the system closes the next window in the order of priority value.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

parameters.description: parameter that is the close priority for the window.
parameters.name: <close_priority>

values.description: Cause the system to display a Save Before Closing message when low memory conditions force a window to be closed, even if a member hasn't clicked on any item in the window. The Save Before Closing message feature is used for newly read mail, gateways, and chat.
The system closes this window first in a low memory condition. (Default priority.)
The system closes windows with these values in ascending order in a low memory condition.

commands.description: fdo$248_Extended $10 (SetUserFont) lets a member make font changes to a field. Once you set a field up this way, a member can change the field's Font Preferences using the Preferences options on the online service Members pull-down menu. This command is used by the online service tools (e.g., the Chat and Mail tools). Issue this command before you create the affected field with fdo$Field16. For more information, see the fdo$Field16 definition.
commands.example_explanation: This example shows that the Mail font ($1025) selected by the member is used for the field selected by fdo$Field16. For instance, if the member selected Times as the Mail font, the Times font is used for the field selected by fdo$Field16.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

parameters.description: parameter that specifies the ID value of the tool containing the font setting the member selects.
parameters.name: <userfont_ID>

commands.description: fdo$248_Extended $07 (ToolQEvent) adds a toolkit event to the queue of pending toolkit events. A toolkit event is an event that the main application handles on behalf of the tool. For example, toolkit event eOnline notifies the applicable tool(s) that a member has signed on to the online service. For more information about toolkit events, see the America Online Macintosh Client Manual.

commands.description:
fdo$248_Extended $1E (TypedDocument) is used in place of fdo$SDocument when typed data is being sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$248_Extended $1E (TypedDocument) creates an empty document window on the client computer screen and suspends processing of the current window. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command.
The text sent by fdo$248_Extended $1E (TypedDocument) can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

commands.description: fdo$248_Extended $1F (TypedMakeMenu) is used in place of FDOS$SMakeMenu when typed data is being sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$248_Extended $1F (TypedMakeMenu) creates a Macintosh hierarchical menu that has specific cluster and ID values assigned to it. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. The text sent by fdo$248_Extended $1F (TypedMakeMenu) can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.

commands.description:
fdo$248_Extended $20 (TypedMenuItem) is used in place of fdo$MenuItem when typed data is sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$248_Extended $20 (TypedMenuItem) adds a menu item to the menu created with fdo$248_Extended $1F (TypedMakeMenu). Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. For more information, see the fdo$248_Extended $1F (TypedMakeMenu) definition.
The text sent by fdo$248_Extended $20 (TypedMenuItem) can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.

fdo$248_Extended $21 (TypedTitle) is used in place of fdo$Title when typed data is sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$248_Extended $21 (TypedTitle) changes the title of the current window to the specified title string.

commands.description:
fdo$break suspends processing of the current form. This command is used when you have a host computer form whose FDO data stream is longer than one packet. fdo$break lets you break up the data stream into more than one packet when it is sent down to the client computer.

Each packet in the FDO stream ends with fdo$break except the last packet, which ends with fdo$enddef.
commands.name: fdo$break

commands.description: fdo$cancel inserts a Cancel button at the specified location. This command defines the button appearance and the action that occurs when the button is selected. When a member presses the Cancel button, the form that contains the button is popped.
commands.example_explanation: This example inserts a Cancel button at the location specified by the character coordinates 1 in the <column> field and 50 in the <row> field.

commands.description:
fdo$canmap maps the COMMAND (88) PERIOD (.) key sequence on a Macintosh or the ESCAPE key on an Apple II or Tandy to a specified field. Normally, the ESCAPE key is mapped to the Cancel button.
Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifiers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
On the Macintosh, if you set the high bit of the field number (field number greater than 127), the fdo$canmap command is ignored. If you specify an invalid field number, the ESCAPE key is disabled on the Apple II.
commands.example_explanation: This example designates field number 2 as the button to be invoked when the member types COMMAND (88) PERIOD (.).
commands.name: fdo$canmap

parameters.description: field identifier number of the button to be invoked when the member types COMMAND (38) PERIOD (.) on a Macintosh or the ESCAPE key on an Apple II or Tandy.
parameters.name: <can_button_field_num>

Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the
first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifiers are
used by the host computer to reference fields.
On the Macintosh, if you set the high bit of the field number (field number
greater than 127), the fdo$canmap command is ignored. If you specify an
invalid field number, the ESCAPE key is disabled on the Apple II.
Syntax
fdo$canmap <can_button_field_num>
<can_button_field_num> 1-byte field identifier number of the button to be
invoked when the member types COMMAND (38)
PERIOD (.) on a Macintosh or the ESCAPE key on an
Apple II or Tandy.
Example
fdo$canmap 2
This example designates field number 2 as the button to be invoked when the
member types COMMAND (88) PERIOD (.).
Platforms
Apple II, Tandy, Macintosh

commands.description:
fdo$canned causes the client software to invoke the specified local database record as a canned form. After the canned form is processed, processing of the current form continues. For example, you might invoke a canned form that defines several buttons and empty text fields. The remainder of the FDO stream might install text into the empty text fields and dispatches into the buttons.
The fdo$canned command lets you use 1,280 canned forms. You can use additional canned forms by issuing an fdo$Switch command with a parameter value of 86 prior to the fdo$canned command.
commands.example_explanation: This command invokes the canned form definition stored in database 15, record 233. After this canned form is processed, the current FDO stream continues processing.
commands.name: fdo$canned

parameters.description: Record number of the canned form record.
parameters.name: <rec_num>

values.description: Valid range for record numbers.

parameters.description: Parameter that is the second digit of the canned form that you want to use. You can only use databases 11 to 15. For example, to use the canned forms in database 11, enter 1 in this field.
parameters.name: <db_num>

values.description: Valid range corresponding to databases 11 through 15.

commands.description: fdo$ClearDispches clears all dispatches associated with the current window. Use this command before a new set of dispatches is sent. commands.description:
fdo$DateTime specifies a date and time to be converted into text by the client software and inserted into a field. The date and time are converted using the user's date and time display preferences and selected time zone.
Note: If the date/time and time zone preferences set on the member's Macintosh are different than those established by this command, the member's Macintosh preferences take precedence.
commands.example_explanation: This example inserts the date and time represented by ($19 $BF $65 SBE) into the current field. The <flags >parameter ($15) specifies that the time is displayed with the time zone and the date in the short format. The result of this u example is "9/8/93 12:43 PM EDT."

commands.description:
fdo$DeleteForm instructs the client software to find and delete the specified window. If the window is not found, no action is taken. Note that if the deleted window is the current window, the top-most window on the client computer screen becomes the new current window.
fdo$DeleteForm is often used to ensure that there is only one window of a given cluster and ID on the client computer screen at any one time.
commands.example_explanation: This example deletes window 101 of the Outbox window cluster ($4F42).
commands.name: fdo$DeleteForm

parameters.description: assigned to identify a particular group of windows. Values for this parameter are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C. This table also lists <window_cluster> names and describes the types of windows they represent.
parameters.name: <window_cluster>

parameters.description: parameter that defines a unique numeric window identifier. Each designated window in an FDO stream is assigned a unique number.
parameters.name: <window_id>

values.description: Valid range for the window identifier.

commands.description:
fdo$dispch defines an action (or dispatch) for a button or list item field. To define a dispatch for a text field, use the fdo$SResetDispch command instead. For more information on dispatches for text fields, see the fdo$ResetDispch definition.

When a member clicks on a field, the field's dispatch determines the action that occurs. A dispatch may cause a form to be retrieved from the client or host computer database. Or a dispatch may cause member-entered data to be sent to the host computer.

A field's dispatch must be placed in the FDO data stream after the field is created.

The first dispatch that occurs in the data stream is assigned to the first field, the second dispatch that occurs is assigned to the second field, etc. If you create a list field that contains one or more selectable items, it must be the last field defined. Problems may occur if a list field is defined first because dispatches belonging to other fields can be erroneously associated with items in the list.

Dispatches vary depending on field type. List fields must have a separate dispatch assigned to each item in the list. Null dispatches (i.e., all parameters of the dispatch equal zero) can be used as placeholders.
commands.name: fdo$dispch
commands.notes: List fields must have a separate dispatch assigned to each item in the list. Null dispatches (i.e., all parameters of the dispatch equal zero) can be used as placeholders.

parameters.description: Menu operation (MOP) code that describes the dispatch associated with the field. Menu operation codes are listed and described in Table E-1 of Appendix E. For more information about common MOP codes, see Appendix E, "Menu Operation (MOP) Codes."
parameters.name: <MOP_code>

parameters.description: A 2-character token and a 2-bit surcharge code which together are encoded into 2 bytes for the dispatch. The token identifies the meaning or purpose of the data packet sent back to the host computer.
parameters.name: (<token><s_code>)

parameters.description: Menu operation information associated with the dispatch.
parameters.name: <MOP_info>

fdo$ResetDispch definition.
When a member clicks on a field, the field's dispatch determines the action that
occurs. A dispatch may cause a form to be retrieved from the client or host
computer database. Or a dispatch may cause member-entered data to be sent to
the host computer.
A field's dispatch must be placed in the FDO data stream after the field is
created.
The first dispatch that occurs in the data stream is assigned to the first field, the
second dispatch that occurs is assigned to the second field, etc. If you create a
list field that contains one or more selectable items, it must be the last field
defined. Problems may occur if a list field is defined first because dispatches
belonging to other fields can be erroneously associated with items in the list.
Dispatches vary depending on field type. List fields must have a separate
dispatch assigned to each item in the list. Null dispatches (i.e., all parameters of
the dispatch equal zero) can be used as placeholders.
Syntax
fdo$dispch <MOP_code> (<token><s_code>) <MOP_info>
<MOP_code> 1-byte menu operation (MOP) code that describes the
dispatch associated with the field. Menu operation
codes are listed and described in Table E-1 of
Appendix E. For more information about common
MOP codes, see Appendix E, "Menu Operation
(MOP) Codes."
(<token><s_code>) 2-character token and a 2-bit surcharge code which
together are encoded into 2 bytes for the dispatch.
The token identifies the meaning or purpose of the
data packet sent back to the host computer. The data

pages.page_footer: America Online Confidential 2-44 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94

metadata.ocr_text:
FDO88 Manual
packet that the token identifies usually contains user
response(s).
The surcharge code indicates whether an action is
allowed from a paid area only, from a free area only,
or from either. If the member triggers a dispatch and
the surcharge code in the dispatch does not match the
current pay status, the client software will offer to
either cancel the dispatch or to change the surcharge
status before executing the dispatch.
For example, if a member is in a paid area and clicks
the Customer Service button whose dispatch has a
surcharge code of "Free", the client software prompts
the member with "Are you certain you want to enter
this free area? Any chat and gateway windows will
be closed. All other windows will be hidden until
you exit." Surcharge code values are as follows:
plus_same Action allowed from paid or free area
plus_pay Action allowed from paid area only
plus_free Action allowed from free area only
<MOP_info> 3 bytes of information that is dependent upon the
value of the MOP code used. <MOP_info> is
divided into as three subparameters: subparm1,
subparm?2, and subparm3.
These subparameter bytes are sometimes taken
together as a multi-byte parameter. For example, if
the MOP code "Ask_DB" is used, the first
subparameter contains the library number and the
second and third subparameter. Taken together, these
subparameters contain the two-byte record number
for the host computer library record that is being
requested.
Example
fdo$dispch 1 ('K1',0) $08 $A8 $5F
This example asks the local database to display the form identified by the 3-
byte library record number shown in the last three parameters (308, SA8 and
$5F).
1 Specifies the MOP code numeric value.

commands.description:
fdo$Domain is used to create a domain, or logical group of windows. Domains let members move between areas, activating any window or menu bar at any time. Domains also inform the host computer if the member changes areas within the online system.
fdo$Domain contains a zero-terminated string that designates the current group of windows. When the member clicks on a button in a window, the client compares the window's domain to the current domain. If the domains match or the old domain is a substring of the current domain, the client software executes the button's dispatch. If the window's domain is different, the client software sends the new domain string to the host computer, marks the dispatch as "pending", and suspends processing until the host computer responds. When the host computer responds, the client software executes the pending dispatch.
commands.example_explanation: This example creates a logical group of windows designated "MGMacintosh Games Forum."
commands.name: fdo$Domain

parameters.description: Variable-length data string sent to the Stratus if the member's domain changes. This is a zero-terminated text argument (i.e., a text string with a 0 appended to it to signify its end).
parameters.name: <sync_data>

fdo$Domain is used to create a domain, or logical group of windows.
Domains let members move between areas, activating any window or menu
bar at any time. Domains also inform the host computer if the member
changes areas within the online system.
fdo$Domain contains a zero-terminated string that designates the current group
of windows. When the member clicks on a button in a window, the client
compares the window's domain to the current domain. If the domains match or
the old domain is a substring of the current domain, the client software executes
the button's dispatch. If the window's domain is different, the client software
sends the new domain string to the host computer, marks the dispatch as
"pending”, and suspends processing until the host computer responds. When
the host computer responds, the client software executes the pending dispatch.
Syntax
fdo$Domain <sync_data>
<sync_data> Variable-length data string sent to the Stratus if the
member's domain changes. This is a zero-terminated
text argument (i.e., a text string with a 0 appended to
it to signify its end).
Example
fdo$Sdomain "MGMacintosh Games Forum"
This example creates a logical group of windows designated "MGMacintosh
Games Forum."
Platforms
Macintosh

commands.description:
fdo$EncodeType overrides the default character encoding that is used when the contents of a text field are sent to the host computer. Fields that contain only plain ASCII data are always sent to the host computer as ASCII.
If fields contain font information (FFSUseFonts field boolean is True), font data is always sent. For more information on field booleans, see Table C.3 in Appendix C.
commands.name: fdo$EncodeType

parameters.description: parameter that identifies the field encoding mask for the current field.
parameters.name: <encode type>

values.description: Sends the contents of the text field in compressed Unicode format, if the field contains non-ASCII data. This is the default behavior.

Sends the contents of the text field using "8-bit text encoding" (remove 8-bit characters from the text and place character substitution instructions in font data), if the field contains only Roman script data.
Sends the contents of the text field using ISO Latin-1 encoding if the field contains only Roman script data.
Sends the contents of the text field in uncompressed Unicode format, if the field contains non-ASCII data.
Sends the contents of the text field in compressed Unicode format, if the field contains non-ASCII data.

<flags> 1-byte that sets field characteristics by directly

(Booleans 1, 2, and 4-8). Flag values are listed in

Examples
Examples of how this parameter is used for the more common field types are
as follows:
Example of Button field
fdo$Field16 Button 0106 0230 0080 0020 $00
fdo$text 00 "Setup"
fdo$dispch AskLocalDB $0000 $00 $05 $85
The first command, fdo$Field16, creates a button field whose upper left corner
is horizontally positioned 106 pixels from the left side of the screen and
vertically positioned 230 pixels from the top of the screen. The button's width
is 80 pixels, height is 20 pixels and no flags are set.
The second command, fdo$text, places the word "Setup" on the button that
was just created.
The third command, fdo$dispch, asks the local database to display the Setup
form identified by the library record number shown in the last three parameters
(800, $05, and $B5).
Example of Text field
fdo$Switch SetNewTE 00
fdo$SetUserFont $1025
fdo$Field16 Text 0075 0112 0420 0161 $D1
fdo$Mark 00 0100
fdo$Switch FieldProtectOn 00
fdo$Switch FieldAccentsOn 00
fdo$Switch SetMainTEField 00
fdo$SetSize 24000
The first command, fdo$Switch, sets the ability to use mixed fonts in the next
text field that is created. If used, the SetNewTE switch must appear before the
text field is created, as in this example:
The second command, fdo$SetUserFont, sets the member font preference to
the one specified by the mail tool. The mail tool's ID is $1025.
The third command, fdo$Field16, creates a text field whose upper left corner is
horizontally positioned 75 pixels from the left side of the screen and vertically

positioned 112 pixels from the top of the screen. The button's width is 420
pixels and height is 161 pixels. The value of $D1 signifies that four flags are
set. The flags are:
* FFSInputRequired (Boolean 1), which requires that the field have input
before the form is sent.
* FFSScroll (Boolean 5), which lets the field be scrollable.
* FFSInput (Boolean 7), which lets the field be editable.
» FFSBox (Boolean 8), which encloses the field with a box.
Note: The first eight booleans can be set using the <flags> parameter of the
fdo$Field16 command.
The fourth command, fdo$Mark, assigns this field an identification number of
100, which is recognized by the mail tool. The mail tool was originally
associated with this form by using the fdo$SetWindowTag command near the
beginning of the data stream.
The fifth command, fdo$Switch, enables the switch that marks the window as
changed if a member makes a change to this field.
The sixth command, fdo$Switch, enables the switch that allows accent
characters and other special characters to be used.
The seventh command, fdo$SSwitch, enables the switch that makes this field
the major field on the form. A status of major affects File Save and Resize
menu options.
The eighth command, fdo$SetSize, limits the number of characters that can be
entered into this text field to 24,000 characters.
Example of List field
fdo$Field16 List 0020 0029 0270 0192 $90
fdo$mline $02 "Online Service Products Center"
fdo$dispch 129 ('K1',0) $02 $C3 S$F9

horizontally positioned 20 pixels from the left side of the screen and vertically
positioned 29 pixels from the top of the screen. The list's width is 270 pixels
and height is 192 pixels. The value of $90 signifies that the list is boxed
(Boolean 8) and scrollable (Boolean 5).

Description
fdo$FieldScript lets a member change the default script (alphabetic character
set) for a specified field. The main use of this command is to change the script
a member can use in a text input field.
Syntax
fdo$FieldScript <field #> <script ID>
<field #> 1-byte parameter that identifies the number of the
field to be modified. Values are as follows:
0 Specifies the current field.
1 to 255 Specifies other fields.
<script ID> 2-byte integer parameter that identifies the number of n
the script to be used. Table C.15 in Appendix C lists
the valid <script ID> values and the scripts they
represent. A value of -1 specifies that the script for a
text input field is determined by the first character the
member enters into the field.
Example
fdo$FieldScript 4 5
This example changes the default script for field 4 to Hebrew (5).

Macintosh

commands.description:
fdo$FillText fills a text field, list field, sketchpad® field, tool image field, bitmap field, or data field with the contents of a Macintosh resource. The type of resource used depends on the field type.

Note: To use fdo$FillText, a text field must have the FFSHaveTE flag set with the fdo$Switch command. For more information, see the fdo$Switch definition.

commands.notes: To use fdo$FillText for text fields, the FFSHaveTE flag must be set via the fdo$Switch command.

Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <field_number>

parameters.name: <resource_ID>

commands.description: fdo$GlobalText replaces the global text string specified by the <ID_value> parameter with the <text> parameter text string. A global text string is a memory location in the online service software that stores a text string. This text string can be used later by another form.
commands.example: fdo$GlobalText 3 "To be or not to be, that is the question."
commands.example_explanation: This example specifies that the new global text string to be used by the fdo$prompt command (3) is "To be or not to be, that is the question."
commands.name: fdo$GlobalText

Specifies a new global text string to be used by the fdo$prompt command. For more information, see the fdo$prompt definition.

commands.description:
fdo$Group is used to create a group of radio buttons. Members can select only one field within a radio button group at one time. If the member selects a field within a group, that field is highlighted while the field previously highlighted is unhighlighted. This command is normally used to implement Macintosh-style radio buttons.
Note: Although this field is ordinarily used with radio buttons, it can also be used with icon fields.
commands.name: fdo$Group
commands.notes: Although this field is ordinarily used with radio buttons, it can also be used with icon fields.

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the field number of the first radio button in the group. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <start_field>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the field number of the last radio button in the group. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <end_field>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the field number of the initially selected radio button. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <initial_field>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the field number of the attached Group field. For radio
parameters.name: <attach>

only one field within a radio button group at one time. If the member selects a
field within a group, that field is highlighted while the field previously

Macintosh-style radio buttons.
Note: Although this field is ordinarily used with radio buttons, it can also be
used with icon fields.

the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier
numbers are used by the host computer to reference
fields.

number of the initially selected radio button. Each
field has an associated sequential field identifier
number (for example, the first field defined is field 1,
the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier

fields.

number of the attached Group field. For radio

fdo$Field16 RadioButton 0040 0025 0120 0016 $00

The first 6 commands create 3 labeled radio buttons.
The seventh command creates a field of type “group,” which serves as a
container for the radio group.

commands.description: fdo$MacIcon specifies a Macintosh icon (Macintosh resource type Icon). If the icon is not found, a generic placeholder icon is used. These icons do not have a mask (a specified area to invert when highlighted). When an icon is selected, a box around the icon is highlighted.
commands.name: fdo$MacIcon

parameters.description: parameter that designates the resource ID of the desired icon.
parameters.name: <icon_resid>

parameters.description: parameter that defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the icon. Whether the icon is positioned using character or pixel coordinates is determined as follows: If the flag FFSTitled is False, the icon is positioned using pixel coordinates. A pixel coordinate is the size of a screen pixel. If the flag FFSTitled is True, the icon is positioned using character coordinates. A character coordinate is the width of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <column>

parameters.description: parameter that defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the icon. Whether the icon is positioned using character or pixel coordinates is determined as follows: If the flag FFSTitled is False, the icon is positioned using pixel coordinates. A pixel coordinate is the size of a screen pixel.
parameters.name: <row>

parameters.description: Flags used to determine positioning coordinates (e.g., FFSTitled).
parameters.name: <flags>

values.description: Icon is positioned using pixel coordinates.
Icon is positioned using character coordinates (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).

commands.description:
fdo$MakeWindowCurrent makes the specified marked window the current window (the window affected by the FDO stream). Use this command when you want a window that is already on-screen but is not the front-most window to receive data sent from the host computer.
If the previous FDO stream ended with fdo$enddef, be sure to include another fdo$MakeWindowCurrent command to set the focus to the window you want.
fdo$MakeWindowCurrent does not bring the form to the top of the member's computer screen.
commands.example_explanation: This example makes window ID number 101 of the Outbox cluster ($4F42) the current window (the window affected by the FDO stream).
commands.name: fdo$MakeWindowCurrent

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the window cluster of the window that is updated during receipt of the current data packet. It is assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SSetWindowTag definition. Window_cluster values are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <window_cluster>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the window ID of the window that is updated during receipt of the current data packet. It is a unique, numeric identifier assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition. Values are 0 to 5000.
parameters.name: <window_id>

commands.description: fdo$Mark assigns an identifying mark number to a field. Marks are used internally by the online software to keep track of fields. Certain FDO commands reference fields by mark numbers (e.g., fdo$MarkText). This enables the online software to be independent of field numbers.

fdo$Mark 0
Opcode: 194 ($C2)
Description
fdo$Mark assigns an identifying mark number to a field. Marks are used
internally by the online software to keep track of fields. Certain FDO
commands reference fields by mark numbers (e.g., fdo$MarkText). This
enables the online software to be independent of field numbers.
Syntax
fdo$Mark <field_number> <mark_number>
<field_number> 2-byte integer parameter that designates the field
number of the marked field. Each field has an
associated sequential field identifier number (for
example, the first field defined is field 1, the second
field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used
by the host computer to reference fields.
L
<mark_number> 2-byte integer parameter that designates the mark
number of the specified field.
Example
fdo$Mark 8 41
This example assigns mark number 41 to field 8.
Platforms
Macintosh

fdo$MarkTypedData identifies the type of data that is sent to a field.

pages.explanation:
This example adds a single entry to a newly created menu.
The first command creates a new menu titled Mail positioned to the immediate left of the Windows menu. For more information, see the fdo$MakeMenu definition.
The second command adds a single entry to the new menu.

Parameter Breakdown for fdo$Menultem:
- $1025: Sets the cluster and ID for the menu item to the Mail cluster.
- 0050: Sets the menu ID to 50.
- 293 (Note: Text lists '293' in breakdown, code shows '255'): Places the new item at the end of the existing menu commands.
- 00: Specifies that this menu item is always enabled.
- $4D: Sets the command key associated with this item to "M."
- 00: Specifies that this item does not have an attached sub-menu.
- $2F $0000 $00: Assigns a dispatch to this menu item. When this item is selected from the Mail menu, the local database is asked to display the form identified by the last three bytes of the dispatch ($00, $04, $D3).
- $04 $D3: (Part of dispatch identifier)
- "Compose Mail": Sets the name of this menu item to "Compose Mail."

metadata.ocr_text:
Example
FDO88 Manual
argument (i.e., a text string with a 0 appended to it to
signify its end).
fdo$MakeMenu $1025 0050 0000 0000 0000 03 $00 "Mail"
fdo$Menultem $1025 0050 255 00 $4D 00 $2F $0000 $00 $04 $D3 "Compose Mail"
This example adds a single entry to a newly created menu.
The first command creates a new menu titled Mail positioned to the immediate
left of the Windows menu. For more information, see the fdo$MakeMenu
definition.
The second command adds a single entry to the new menu.

commands.description:
fdo$MultiIcon draws the specified Macintosh icon (Macintosh resource type ICN#). If the Macintosh is in color, the FDO engine first looks for a Macintosh resource type of icl8. Ificl8 does not exist with the specified resource ID, the FDO engine then looks for a resource file type of ICN# with the specified resource ID.
fdo$SMultiIcon differs from fdo$MacIcon in that fdo$MultiIcon draws ICN# resources, which include a mask (i.e., a specified area to invert when highlighted), and fdo$MacIcon draw icons of type Icon, which do not have a mask. For more information, see the fdo$MacIcon definition.
commands.name: fdo$SMultiIcon

parameters.description: parameter that designates the resource ID of the multi-icon. This is a flipped integer argument.
parameters.name: <resid>

parameters.description: parameter that defines the horizontal position of the upper left comer of the icon in pixel coordinates if the flag FFSTitled is not set. If FFSTitled is set, it defines the horizontal position of the bottom center of the icon in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide). This is a flipped integer argument.
parameters.name: <column>

parameters.description: parameter that defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the icon in pixel coordinates if the flag FFSTitled is not set. If FFSTitled is set, it defines the vertical position of the bottom center of the icon in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide). This is a flipped integer argument.
parameters.name: <row>

parameters.description: Flag parameter referenced in column and row definitions (e.g., FFSTitled).
parameters.name: <flags>

values.description: When set, defines position as the bottom center of the icon in character coordinates rather than upper left corner in pixel coordinates.

the specified resource ID.
fdo$SMultiIcon differs from fdo$MacIcon in that fdo$MultiIcon draws

commands.description:
fdo$NoAutoShowWindow prevents the client software from automatically deciding when to show a window. This command is rarely used and is intended as an alternate solution in case the client software's built-in algorithm breaks down and the window is drawn before all of its fields are defined.

Note: To use the fdo$AutoShowWindow command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $0A form of the command in your code.

Issue this command immediately after you issue the command that creates the window (e.g., fdo$Window16) and before an fdo$SetWindowTag command.

If you issue an fdo$NoAutoShowWindow command, you must also issue an fdo$SResizeWindowNow command when it is appropriate to show the window. Otherwise, the window is not drawn until you issue the fdo$enddef command. For more information, see the fdo$ResizeWindowNow definition.
commands.name: fdo$NoAutoShowWindow
commands.notes: To use the fdo$AutoShowWindow command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $0A form of the command in your code. Issue this command immediately after you issue the command that creates the window (e.g., fdo$Window16) and before an fdo$SetWindowTag command. If you issue an fdo$NoAutoShowWindow command, you must also issue an fdo$SResizeWindowNow command when it is appropriate to show the window. Otherwise, the window is not drawn until you issue the fdo$enddef command.

commands.description:
fdo$Notify is used during the definition of a window. It specifies a string to be sent to the host computer when the current window is closed, if that window is also the last window of its cluster. The window containing fdo$SNotify must have a non-0 cluster specified via an fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition.
fdo$Notify is used in gateway windows. When a member exits the gateway, the host computer must be notified so that the gateway connection can be closed.
commands.example_explanation: This example sends the string "CG" to the host computer.
commands.name: fdo$Notify

parameters.description: Designates the text to be sent to the host computer. The length of this parameter is determined by the length byte of this command. The Notify string must begin with a token value. The client software escapes this string and sends it to the host computer.
parameters.name: <message>

commands.description: fdo$Picture draws the specified Macintosh picture resource on the client computer screen. Because pictures are considered fields, they must have dispatches assigned to them. parameters.description: Parameter that designates the Macintosh picture resource number of the picture to be drawn on the form. If the resource number specified is a valid resource number with the quantity 10,000 added to it, the picture is drawn in color. This is a flipped integer argument.

parameters.description: Parameter that defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the picture in pixel coordinates if the flag FFSTitled is not set. If FFSTitled is set, it defines the horizontal position of the bottom center of the picture in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <column>

parameters.description: Parameter that defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the picture in pixel coordinates if the flag FFSTitled is not set. If FFSTitled is set, it defines the vertical position of the bottom center of the picture in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <row>

parameters.description: Flag parameter referenced in column and row definitions (e.g., FFSTitled).
parameters.name: <pic_flags>

0 fdo$Picture
Opcode: 154 ($9A)
Description
FDO88 Manual
fdo$Picture draws the specified Macintosh picture resource on the client
computer screen. Because pictures are considered fields, they must have
dispatches assigned to them. For more information on assigning dispatches to
fields, see the fdo$dispch definition.
Pictures, if not disabled, can be selected by a member and are highlighted when
selected.
Syntax
fdo$Picture <pic_id> <column> <row> <pic_flags>
<pic_id>
<column>
<row>

commands.description: fdo$PlusGroup specifies a plus group number for a form and makes the specified plus group the current plus group. Optionally, you can send this new plus group number back to the host computer. For more information about plus groups, see the America Online manual Building an Online Service.
commands.example_explanation: This example assigns plus group number 100 to a form that resides on the member's drive, and sends this value to the host computer (1).
commands.name: fdo$PlusGroup

parameters.description: Specifies whether or not the new plus group number is sent to the host computer. Values are as follows:
parameters.name: <send_flag>

values.description: Sends the new plus group number to the host computer. Specify this value when the form you want resides on the member's drive.
Does not send the new plus group value to the host computer. Specify this value when the form you want is sent from the host computer.

commands.description: fdo$pop pops a specified number of forms from the client computer window. This command is not normally used within Macintosh FDO streams. If used on the Macintosh, it is used only on modal windows, since the member can re-order modeless windows at any time.
commands.example_explanation: This example pops 1 form from the client computer window.

commands.description: fdo$prompt substitutes for an fdo$text command. It places the global prompt string in the most recently defined text field. Various commands, including fdo$Switch, can change the value of the global prompt string.
commands.example_explanation:
The defined global prompt string is placed on this form for this field. For example, if the global prompt string is defined on a previous form (using fdo$GlobalText) as "Are you sure you want to sign off and quit?" that string appears on this form. For more information, see the fdo$GlobalText definition.
The global prompt string can also be changed by an online service client tool. For more information about client tools, see the America Online Macintosh Client Manual.

commands.description:
fdo$RememberSizeID assigns a resize ID to the current window. This resize ID lets a member save a window's size by selecting Remember Window Size from the Window menu. The next time a window with that resize ID is created, it is shown at the remembered size. Assign resize IDs centrally to avoid conflict.

Note: To use the fdo$RememberSizeID command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $08 form of the command in your code.

Issue fdo$RememberSizeID immediately after you issue the command that creates the window (e.g., fdo$Window16).

Windows that do not contain an fdo$RememberSizeID command but do have a cluster assigned using fdo$SetWindowTag also have Remember Window Size enabled. These windows use the cluster and ID as an alternate form of the remember size ID function. For windows that have both a cluster and a resize ID, the resize ID takes precedence.
commands.name: fdo$RememberSizeID
commands.notes: To use the fdo$RememberSizeID command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $08 form of the command in your code. Issue fdo$RememberSizeID immediately after you issue the command that creates the window (e.g., fdo$Window16). Windows that do not contain an fdo$RememberSizeID command but do have a cluster assigned using fdo$SetWindowTag also have Remember Window Size enabled. These windows use the cluster and ID as an alternate form of the remember size ID function. For windows that have both a cluster and a resize ID, the resize ID takes precedence.

commands.description:
fdo$RememberSizeIDLong is identical to fdo$RememberSizeID, except that it accepts a long integer resize ID. This command must only be used to assign global ID as a resize ID. Global IDs are in a range that are different than the resize IDs derived from cluster and ID.

Windows that do not have a resize ID specified with fdo$RememberSizelD use the window's cluster and ID as a 32-bit resize ID. For more information, see the fdo$RememberSizeID definition.
commands.example_explanation: This example establishes the ID to be used for remembering the window's size as 10.
commands.name: fdo$RememberSizeIDLong

parameters.description: parameter that designates the ID used for remembering the window's size.
parameters.name: <resize_ID>

commands.description:
fdo$reset resets the application. For the Apple II and the Tandy, all menus and open forms are cleared from the client computer screen.
For the Macintosh, the application is reset to its default state (i.e., logging on or off, not uploading or downloading, no active hotkeys). This is often used before a member enters a chat room.
commands.example_explanation: This example specifies a reset resulting from a chat room entry.
commands.name: fdo$reset

parameters.description: identifies the circumstances of the reset. Values are follows:
parameters.name: <reset_type>

values.description: Specifies a reset resulting from a chat room entry.
Specifies a reset not resulting from a chat room entry.

commands.description:
If you issue an fdo$ResizeWindowNow command, you must also issue an fdo$NoAutoShowWindow command when it is appropriate to show the window. Otherwise, the window is not drawn until you issue the fdo$enddef command. For more information, see the fdo$NoAutoShowWindow definition.

Note: To use the fdo$ResizeWindowNow command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $0B form of the command in your code.

Issue the fdo$SResizeWindowNow command after the last field is defined and before content is installed into the form. That way, the client software has all the information it needs to show the window's fields in the correct places, and the member can see the window's contents as they are defined.

your code.

before content is installed into the form. That way, the client software has all
the information it needs to show the window's fields in the correct places, and
the member can see the window's contents as they are defined.

commands.description:
fdo$ResetDispch sets the dispatch of the specified field. This is used to change the dispatch of a button.
For example, the Next button on a Message Board contains the dispatch value of the next record. Therefore, you must change the dispatch value of the Next button to reflect the next record number.
For list fields, the dispatch is added to the list of dispatches for the list. This command can also be used to assign a dispatch to a text field.
commands.example_explanation: This example changes the dispatch associated with field 3. When this field is selected, the local database is asked to display the form identified by the library record number in the last three parameters of the dispatch ($00, $05, and $B5).

commands.description:
fdo$SetClosePriority sets a priority value for the current window. This value is used when the client software needs to close open window(s) because of a low memory condition.
Note: To use the fdo$SetClosePriority command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $12 form of the command in your code.
When the client software needs more memory, the system closes windows in the order of priority value, starting at 0, then 1, then 2, etc. (regardless of whether they are positive or negative) until the memory problem is alleviated. If a member refuses to close a window after receiving a request to do so, the system closes the next window in the order of priority value.
commands.name: fdo$SetClosePriority
commands.notes: To use the fdo$SetClosePriority command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $12 form of the command in your code.

parameters.description: parameter that is the close priority for the window.
parameters.name: <close_priority>

values.description: Causes the system to display a Save Before Closing message when low memory conditions force a window to be closed, even if a member hasn't clicked on any item in the window. The Save Before Closing message feature is used for newly read mail, gateways, and chat.
The system closes this window first in a low memory condition. (Default priority.)
The system closes windows with these values in ascending order in a low memory condition.

commands.description:
fdo$SetWindowTag marks a window for later use. The window is marked by assigning it a descriptive window cluster name and a unique window identification number.
A marked window can be accessed (i.e., have text put in it), even though it is not the top-most window on the client computer screen. The API also uses window clusters and window IDs to identify windows.
An example of a marked window is a FlashNote. A FlashNote is a window that can be received and then covered when another window is received. Text meant for the FlashNote window would spill into the current window if not for the ability to mark the FlashNote window. fdo$MakeWindowCurrent makes the marked window the current window so text meant for the FlashNote window is received properly. For more information, see the fdo$MakeWindowCurrent definition.
Window clusters are also used to place a set of windows and menus in a logical group. The logical group can then be closed as a group using fdo$CloseCluster. In addition, the online software automatically closes all menus in the logical group when the last window of the logical group is closed. For more information, see the fdo$CloseCluster definition.
commands.name: fdo$SetWindowTag

parameters.description: Parameter that designates a particular group of windows. Window_cluster values are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <window_cluster>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates a unique numeric window identifier. Values are 0 to 5000.
parameters.name: <window_id>

not the top-most window on the client computer screen. The API also uses
window clusters and window IDs to identify windows.
An example of a marked window is a FlashNote. A FlashNote is a window
that can be received and then covered when another window is received. Text
meant for the FlashNote window would spill into the current window if not for
the ability to mark the FlashNote window. fdo$MakeWindowCurrent makes
the marked window the current window so text meant for the FlashNote window
is received properly. For more information, see the fdo$MakeWindowCurrent
definition.
Window clusters are also used to place a set of windows and menus in a logical
group. The logical group can then be closed as a group using fdo$CloseCluster.
In addition, the online software automatically closes all menus in the logical
group when the last window of the logical group is closed. For more
information, see the fdo$CloseCluster definition.
Syntax
fdo$SetWindowTag <window_cluster> <window_id>
<window_cluster> 2-byte integer parameter that designates a particular
group of windows. Window_cluster values are listed in
Table C.13 in Appendix C.
<window_id> 2-byte integer parameter that designates a unique
numeric window identifier. Values are 0 to 5000.
Example
fdo$Window16 -0001 -0001 0500 0290 $11
fdo$SetWindowTag $1025 0030

commands.description: fdo$SndName plays the sound resource file with the specified name. The Macintosh must contain a visible sound resource that contains the specified sound in order to play the sound. A sound resource is visible if it is in at least one of the following locations: System file of the System Folder, Online Sounds file, Online One file, Online Two file, Online Three file, Resource file that has been opened by the member. Sound resources are played synchronously; that is, they are played before the system continues with its current operation. For this reason, sounds must be kept short.

commands.description: fdo$Switch sets a software switch to a specified value.
commands.name: fdo$Switch

parameters.description: Parameter that specifies the software switch that affects the window or field that is set. Switches 1 to 99 modify the window display. These switches are listed in Table C.2 in Appendix C. Switches 100 to 255 modify the field specified in the <switch_parameter> variable. Switches 101 to 163 are used to directly set field flags (i.e., booleans). These flags are specified in FDO command syntax statements with a <flags> argument and are listed in Tables C.3 and C.4 in Appendix C. Switches 101 to 140 directly set the specified flag to True, while switches 141 to 179 set the specified flag to False. Switches that modify the specified field are listed in Table B.4 in Appendix B.
parameters.name: <switch_number>

parameters.description: Parameter that specifies a switch parameter value. This is normally used to specify a field number value. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields. A value of 0 designates the next field that is created.
parameters.name: <switch_parameter>

values.description: Designates the next field that is created.

fdo$Text also places list items in a list field. Each fdo$Text command creates a new entry in the list field. Each of these entries must, therefore, have its own dispatch. For more information, see the fdo$dispch definition.

commands.description:
fdo$TypedDocument is used in place of fdo$Document when typed data is being sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$TypedDocument creates an empty document window on the client computer screen and suspends processing of the current form. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command.

Note: To use the fdo$TypedDocument command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $1E form of the command in your code.

The text sent by fdo$TypedDocument can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.

Opcode: 248 ($F8)
Sub-Opcode: 30 ($1E)
Description
fdo$TypedDocument is used in place of fdo$Document when typed data is
being sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$TypedDocument creates an
empty document window on the client computer screen and suspends
processing of the current form. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed
Unicode types are meaningful to this command.
Note: To use the fdo$TypedDocument command with the form_edit
tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $1E form of the command in
your code.
The text sent by fdo$TypedDocument can be in any defined language or
script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the
member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has
chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English
equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.
Syntax
fdo$TypedDocument $1E <flags> <type> <data>
<flags> 1-byte parameter that determines if the resulting
window can be edited and if the window is created
using a monospaced font. Values are as follows:
$01 Creates a window that can be edited.
$08 Creates a window that uses a
monospaced font.
$09 Creates a window that can be edited
and uses a monospaced font.
<type> 2-byte integer parameter that identifies the type.
Values are as follows:
$0000 ASCII

commands.description:
fdo$TypedMenuItem is used in place of fdo$MenuItem when typed data is sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$TypedMenuItem adds a menu item to the menu created with fdo$TypedMakeMenu. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. For more information, see the fdo$TypedMakeMenu definition.

Note: To use the fdo$TypedMenuItem command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $20 form of the command in your code.

The text sent by fdo$TypedMenuItem can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.

commands.description:
fdo$UseDisplay checks to see if a window with the specified cluster and ID exists. If so, that window is brought to the front and the processing of the current FDO stream stops. If the window does not exist, no action is taken, and the remainder of the FDO stream is processed.
This command is useful when you want one copy of a given form to be displayed, such as the help form or the welcome form. You must first define a cluster and ID using fdo$SetWindowTag. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition.
commands.example_explanation: This example brings window ID number 100 of the Chat cluster (S4343) to the front.
commands.name: fdo$UseDisplay

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the window cluster of the window that is brought to the front. It is assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition. Window_cluster values are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <window_cluster>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the window that is brought to the front. It is a unique, numeric identifier assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition.
parameters.name: <window_id>
