Description
fdo$UseDisplay checks to see if a window with the specified cluster and ID
exists. If so, that window is brought to the front and the processing of the
current FDO stream stops. If the window does not exist, no action is taken,
and the remainder of the FDO stream is processed.
This command is useful when you want one copy of a given form to be
displayed, such as the help form or the welcome form. You must first define a
cluster and ID using fdo$SetWindowTag. For more information, see the
fdo$SetWindowTag definition.

<window_cluster> 2-byte integer parameter that designates the window

assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For
more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag
definition. Window_cluster values are listed in Table
C.13 in Appendix C.
<window_id> 2-byte integer parameter that designates the window
that is brought to the front. It is a unique, numeric
identifier assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag
command. For more information, see the
fdo$SetWindowTag definition.
Example
fdo$UseDisplay $4343 100
This example brings window ID number 100 of the Chat cluster (S4343) to the
front.

commands.description:
fdo$UseRelativeRect positions the next field that is received relative to the specified field.
fdo$field is used to position fields absolutely.
commands.example_explanation: This example positions the upper left hand corner of the next field that is received 2304 pixels horizontally and 2 pixels vertically relative to field number 2.
commands.name: fdo$UseRelativeRect

parameters.description: Designates the field to which the next field received is relatively placed. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <field_number>

parameters.description: Specifies the horizontal location in pixels of the upper left hand corner of the next field received. This location is relative to the horizontal location of the field specified in <field_number>. A value of 0 arranges fields in the same row.
parameters.name: <relative_x>

values.description: Arranges fields in the same row

parameters.description: Specifies the vertical location in pixels of the upper left hand corner of the next field received. This location is relative to the vertical location of the field specified in <field_number>. A value of 0 arranges fields in the same column.
parameters.name: <relative_y>

values.description: Arranges fields in the same column

FDOB88 Manual
fdo$UseRelativeRect
Opcode: 164 ($SA4)
Description
fdo$UseRelativeRect positions the next field that is received relative to the
specified field.
fdo$field is used to position fields absolutely.
Syntax
fdo$UseRelativeRect <field_number> <relative_x> <relative_y>
<field_number> 1-byte parameter that designates the field to which the
next field received is relatively placed. Each field has
an associated sequential field identifier number (for
example, the first field defined is field 1, the second
field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are
used by the host computer to reference fields.
<relative_x> 2-byte integer parameter that specifies the horizontal
location in pixels of the upper left hand comner of the
next field received. This location is relative to the
horizontal location of the field specified in
<field_number>. A value of 0 arranges fields in the
same row.
<relative_y> 2-byte integer parameter that specifies the vertical
location in pixels of the upper left hand comer of the
next field received. This location is relative to the
vertical location of the field specified in
<field_number>. A value of 0 arranges fields in the
same column.
Example
fdo$UseRelativeRect 2 2304 2
This example positions the upper left hand corner of the next field that is received
2304 pixels horizontally and 2 pixels vertically relative to field number 2.

commands.description: . fdo$window creates a window on the client computer screen using pixel coordinates. This command follows fdo$start. If you do not specify a title, the window is titled "UNTITLED." Windows can also be defined with character coordinates using fdo$window16.
commands.name: fdo$window

parameters.description: Defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the window in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <column>

values.description: Centers the window horizontally and vertically.
Causes this parameter to be ignored.

parameters.description: Defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the window in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).

values.description: Causes <column> to be ignored and each new window to be staggered below and to the right of the previous window.
Causes the system to reset window staggering and place this window in the upper left of the screen.

parameters.description: Defines the width of the window in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <width>

commands.description: fdo$Swtype changes the type of the last window sent to the client software for the Apple II and the Tandy. This command, in conjunction with fdo$mline, creates a navigation window. For more information, see the fdo$Smline definition.
commands.example_explanation: This example changes the last window sent to the client software to a navigation window.
commands.name: fdo$Swtype

parameters.description: identifies the action to be taken on the last window or menu received, or provides instruction on whether a menu or window is to be created.
parameters.name: <action>

values.description: Specifies a menu bar.
Specifies a navigation window on the Macintosh or PCAOL. Specifies a menu bar pull-down menu on the Apple II or PC-Link. This is the only valid value on a Macintosh.

fdo$wtype
Opcode: 14 (S0E)
Description
fdo$Swtype changes the type of the last window sent to the client software for
the Apple II and the Tandy. This command, in conjunction with fdo$mline,
creates a navigation window. For more information, see the fdo$Smline
definition.
Syntax
fdo$wtype <action>
<action> 1-byte parameter that identifies the action to be taken
on the last window or menu received, or provides
instruction on whether a menu or window is to be
created. Values are as follows:
0 Specifies a menu bar.
1 Specifies a navigation window on the
Macintosh or PCAOL. Specifies a
menu bar pull-down menu on the
Apple II or PC-Link. This is the only
valid value on a Macintosh.
Example
fdo$wtype 1
This example changes the last window sent to the client software to a navigation
window.
Platforms
Apple II, Macintosh, Tandy

APPENDIX A
Glossary
base FDO FDO command that does
not have its high bit set. Base FDO
commands have no byte count arguments
associated with them. (See byte-count
FDO.)
byte argument FDO command
argument that is one byte in length.
byte count Total length of the data
accompanying the FDO byte-count
argument.
byte-count FDO FDO command
that has its high bit set. A byte-count FDO
command has a byte count argument
associated with it that indicates the number
of bytes of data associated with the
command.
character Client computer screen
coordinates in which the screen is divided
into a finite number of columns and rows.
Using character coordinates, items can be
positioned on the screen in increments of
the size of one column or row. On the
Apple II text display screen, the size of
columns and rows is based on the size of
one character.
C-style text string Alphanumeric
text string that is terminated with a zero.
current window Window into which
data is being sent.
America Online Confidential A-1
dispatch Six-byte set of parameters
designating an action to be performed by
the host or client computer.
domain Grouping of related windows
and/or menus used by the Stratus to track
user location.
FDO language Acronymn for
Form Definition Opcode language, which
is made up of commands that control the
appearance or functionality of an online
service form/window.
field Smallest functional unit on a
form. Field types include buttons, input
fields, pictures, icons, lists, and text fields.
See Table C.2 in Appendix C for a
complete list of fields.
field number Sequential field
identifier assigned to each field in the order
it is defined (i.e., the first field defined is
field 1, the second field defined is field 2).
The field number is specified in FDO
command syntax statements by the
<field_number > argument.
finder icon Icon that is selectable and
has a centered title underneath it.
flipped integer FDO parameter that
is two bytes in length. The integer contains
a high byte and a low byte. They are
normally transmitted high byte to low byte.
Machines with a 6502 microprocessor (i.e.,
Apple II, Commodore C64) interpret

Index
January 1994
integer data in a flipped manner, low byte
to high byte. Many FDO command
definitions contain flipped integer
arguments.
form Individual window display.
Forms may contain any number of fields
and field types.
integer argument FDO command
parameter that requires two bytes of
storage. The integer's value, divided by
256, is stored in the high byte of the
integer's value. The remainder of this
division is stored in the low byte.
invisible window Window that is
added to the menu option titled "Window"
when closed.
MOP Acronymn for Menu Operation
Code, which occupies the first two bytes of
a dispatch. The MOP defines what action
occurs when the user selects the field
associated with the dispatch.
modal Macintosh window to which the
user must respond. When a modal
window is opened, the menu bar is
inactive, and the user cannot activate any
other windows.
modeless Macintosh window scheme
in which the user may at any time activate
another window or menu bar option.
multi-icon Icon with a mask. A mask
is the area that is highlighted when the
multi-icon is highlighted.
online user Online service member
currently using one of the
telecommunications networks that the
online service supports.
command period Keystroke
sequence (pressing the COMMAND key and
the PERIOD key simultaneously) used to
abort/cancel the current action.
pixel coordinates Associated with
where the window is placed on the
computer monitor. One increment equals
one pixel. One pixel equals 1/72".
pop Action of clearing a form/window
from the screen of the client computer.
resource Block of data identified by a
four-character resource type and an integer
resource ID. Resources are stored on
Macintosh disks.
resource ID Identification number
assigned to a Macintosh resource. The
resource ID is then used to call the object
during a software routine. (See also
resource.)
script Collection of any number of
individual forms comprising an online
dialog that presents information or asks
questions.
synchronous sounds Played in
full before the system resumes its current
operation. Synchronous sounds must be
kept short so that software functionality is
not slowed.
Text Edit Built-in Macintosh text
editor.
top-most window Currently active
window. The top-most window has an
active title bar and is normally the window
last selected by the user.
variable byte Variable-length FDO
command parameter. Its length is
determined by the amount of information
being sent. A string (S*) parameter, which
is used on fields where a user can enter
varying amounts of data, is an example of a
variable-length parameter.
window Area on the client computer
screen that defines the bounds of the form.

samples.

subsections.content: (FDO data stream source is local database 4, Resource ID 211.)

Figure B.5 – America Online Welcome Screen

[Visual Description of Figure]:
A screenshot of the 'America Online' welcome screen.
Header: 'Welcome, USERNAME!'
Left Panel:
- Logo: 'AMERICA Online'
- Icons: 'New Services', 'You have mail'
- Button: 'Departments'
Right Panel (Content):
1. Important Information for All Members: America Online Lowers Prices! Read the Letter from Steve Case for details.
2. Learn and talk about alternative medicine, health reform, disabilities and more in the Better Health & Medical Forum!
3. Investors! Check out the Decision Point Alert weekly market letter & get valuable investing tips in the Stock Market Timing & Charts area.
4. TOP NEWS STORY: PRES. CLINTON WILL ACCEPT DEEPER BUDGET CUTS FOR LEAVING BUDGET INTACT
Interactive Elements: Multiple 'Click here' buttons associated with content blocks.

metadata.ocr_text:
APPENDIX C
Reference Tables
This appendix includes 14 tables that define the different parameters used by
the FDO commands. Each table contains all valid values for that parameter and
a description of each value's effect if used in an FDO data stream. Each table is
preceded by a brief description of its purpose and content. Where applicable,
brief explanations of parameters are also provided.
Values are presented in the most common form in which they are used. In
some cases, such as in Table C.2, Field Types, both the numeric and text
values of the argument are shown since you can specify either value for the
parameter when you build a form. For any numeric value, you can use either
decimal or hexadecimal format.
Window Types
Table C.1 presents values for the <window_type> parameter used with the
fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command. Values from 1 to 8 are only
applicable to the Macintosh. Macintosh window types are illustrated in
Appendix F, “Macintosh Window Types.”
Table C.1 Window Types
ModalStandard Standard, double line border modal window.
ModelessRound Round-edged, modeless window with close
box and no resize.
Standard Normal window with grow and zoom boxes.
ModalShadow Modal window with drop shadow.
ModalSingleBorder Modal window with single line border.
Modeless window with no close box.
America Online Confidential C-1 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94

This appendix includes 14 tables that define the different parameters used by the FDO commands. Each table contains all valid values for that parameter and a description of each value's effect if used in an FDO data stream. Each table is preceded by a brief description of its purpose and content. Where applicable, brief explanations of parameters are also provided.

Values are presented in the most common form in which they are used. In some cases, such as in Table C.2, Field Types, both the numeric and text values of the argument are shown since you can specify either value for the parameter when you build a form. For any numeric value, you can use either decimal or hexadecimal format.

rows.col3: SketchImage
rows.col4: Field used for vector drawing commands.

rows.col3: Holder
rows.col4: Place-holding field used to store data not visible to the member.

rows.col3: Toolkit
rows.col4: Field maintained and drawn by an online service tool.

rows.col3: Bitmap
rows.col4: Field that contains bitmap data of the Apple data type BitMap. The contents of a Bitmap field is set with fdo$SetData commands or via fdo$FillText which fills the bitmap field with the contents of a 'Btmp' resource.

rows.col3: PopButton
rows.col4: Special field, like a popup menu, that does not have a title and can contain a separate dispatch for each selected item.

rows.col3: ScrollToolkit
rows.col4: Field that is a variation of the Toolkit field where the main application maintains horizontal and/or vertical scroll-bars and notifies the tool when the member scrolls the field.

rows.col3: ToolScrollImage
rows.col4: Field that is a variation of the ToolImage field, where the main application maintains horizontal and/or vertical scrollbars and notifies the tool when the member scrolls the field.

Multiplexed Field Booleans
When a field is specified using the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command, there
are 37 booleans associated with it. The state of the boolean (True/on or
False/off) can be set in two different ways. For this reason, this table is split
into two parts.
Table C.3 presents the first eight booleans, which are usually set by the <flags>
parameter of the fdo$MacIcon, fdo$Field, or fdo$Field16 command (but can
be set using the fdo$Switch command). :
The first eight booleans are multiplexed, which means they have a different
meaning depending on the field type (text, button, list, etc.). The remaining
booleans are presented in Table C.4.
When you build a form, you set the first eight booleans by using their
hexadecimal value. The total value determines which of the seven flag(s) are
set. For example, a value of $90 means that Boolean 5, which has a
hexadecimal value of $10, and Boolean 8, which has a hexadecimal value of
$80, are set.

Flips a radio box or check box field's default value to its other value. If the radio box or check box is on, it is turned off and vice versa.
Note that for list fields, FFSFlip sets up the field so that pressing RETURN has the same result as double-clicking on the selected list item. Use of this flag is not recommended since the member has no visual indication of what the RETURN key will do. A better user interface is to add a default button to the form and put a Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18) on the button that points to the list field. This has the same effect as causing the RETURN key to invoke the selected list item and gives the member a clear indication of what will happen.

Field Switch Values
The fdo$Switch command can be used with either the fdo$Field or
fdo$Field16 command or the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command.
Table C.5 presents the values used for the <switch_number> parameter of the
fdo$Switch command.
Switch values 101 through 180 affect the field booleans described in Table C.3
and C.4. Switch values 101 through 140 turn on field booleans 1 to 40,
respectively; switch values 141 through 180 turn off field booleans 1 to 40,
respectively.
Although all switch values 101 through 180 change the state of a corresponding
field boolean, not all of these switch values are useful. For example, switches
110 and 150 are not meaningful because they would change the state of a text
field's FFSHaveTE boolean (Boolean 10) which is a read-only boolean.
When you build a form, use the numeric or text value. When a form is
decompiled using the FDO forms compiler, the numeric value for this
parameter is displayed.
For more information, see the definition of the fdo$Switch command in
Chapter 2.
Most often the fdo$Switch command is placed immediately after the
fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command in the FDO data stream; however there
are a few cases where it must be placed before.

sections.content:
The fdo$Switch command can be used with either the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command or the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command. Table C.5 presents the values used for the <switch_number> parameter of the fdo$Switch command.

Switch values 101 through 180 affect the field booleans described in Table C.3 and C.4. Switch values 101 through 140 turn on field booleans 1 to 40, respectively; switch values 141 through 180 turn off field booleans 1 to 40, respectively.

Although all switch values 101 through 180 change the state of a corresponding field boolean, not all of these switch values are useful. For example, switches 110 and 150 are not meaningful because they would change the state of a text field's FFSHaveTE boolean (Boolean 10) which is a read-only boolean.

When you build a form, use the numeric or text value. When a form is decompiled using the FDO forms compiler, the numeric value for this parameter is displayed.

For more information, see the definition of the fdo$Switch command in Chapter 2.

Most often the fdo$Switch command is placed immediately after the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command in the FDO data stream; however there are a few cases where it must be placed before.

Appendix C: Reference Tables January 1994
Table C.5 Field Switch Values (Continued)
Switch Text Value Description
SetEnterKeyField Maps the ENTER (or COMMAND-RETURN)
key(s) to the specified field.
If the member presses ENTER (or COMMAND-
RETURN), the AOL software acts as though
the specified field was clicked. This switch is
used by the Send Now button on the
Compose Mail form and the Send button on
the Instant Message form.
Window Switch Values
Table C.6 lists the values used by the <switch_number> parameter of the
fdo$Switch command. They are used to make changes to the current window.
When you build a form, use these values for the fdo$Switch command when it
follows the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command.
Some switches have different numeric and text values for their true and false
states. For these types of switches, both the numerical and text values are
provided, with the true setting appearing first. The associated switch
description is for the true setting (see Switch 1 and 2).
When you build a form, use the numeric value of the switch. When a form is
decompiled using the FDO forms compiler, only the numeric value for this
parameter is displayed.
Table C.5 presents other switch values, but those are used by the fdo$Switch
command to make changes to the current field in a form.
Note: In most cases, the fdo$Switch command is placed immediately after the
fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command in the FDO data stream; however
there are some exceptions.
~ America Online Confidential C-20 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94

sections.content:
Switch Text Value Description
SetEnterKeyField Maps the ENTER (or COMMAND-RETURN) key(s) to the specified field. If the member presses ENTER (or COMMAND-RETURN), the AOL software acts as though the specified field was clicked. This switch is used by the Send Now button on the Compose Mail form and the Send button on the Instant Message form.

Table C.6 lists the values used by the <switch_number> parameter of the fdo$Switch command. They are used to make changes to the current window.
When you build a form, use these values for the fdo$Switch command when it follows the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command.
Some switches have different numeric and text values for their true and false states. For these types of switches, both the numerical and text values are provided, with the true setting appearing first. The associated switch description is for the true setting (see Switch 1 and 2).
When you build a form, use the numeric value of the switch. When a form is decompiled using the FDO forms compiler, only the numeric value for this parameter is displayed.
Table C.5 presents other switch values, but those are used by the fdo$Switch command to make changes to the current field in a form.

228. (kMMFlagTrueStart-
kMMFlagFalseEnd).
IDs for global menu flags are as follows:
* Global menu flag 1 = True, causes menu
items with a menu flag value of 101
(kMMFlagTrueStart) to be enabled.
* Global menu flag 1 = False, dims menu
items with a menu flag value of 101.
* Global menu flag 2 = True, causes menu
items with a menu flag value of 102
(kMMFlagTrueStart+1) to be enabled.
* Global menu flag 2 = False, dims menu
items with a menu flag value of 102.

rows.col1: SetWarnDisplay / NoWarnDisplay: If True, causes the warning 'This window contains unsaved data. Do you want to close it?' to appear when the member closes the window.
Note: This flag is not very useful, since it does not check to see if the window has been changed and not saved.

SetWarnTchDisplay / NoWarnTchDisplay: If True, causes the warning 'This window contains unsaved data. Do you want to close it?' to appear when the member attempts to close the window after the window has been changed or touched.
SetOnlineOnlyDisplay / NoOnlineOnlyDisplay: If True, causes the window to be closed when the member goes from online to offline or vice versa.
SetMBOnlyDisplay / NoMBOnlyDisplay: If True, causes the window to be closed if the mailbox file is closed.
SuppressAborts / NoSuppressAborts: If True, aborts (COMMAND PERIOD) are ignored.
SetMarkSendDisplay / NoMarkSendDisplay: If True, sets the Mark send flag for the window, which causes the client to send mark numbers to the host instead of field numbers in Send_Form dispatch.
SetResizeSwitches: Sets various window resizing options, depending on the value of the ID. Available ID values are as follows:
ID 1 - Deletes the window's maximum width
ID 2 - Deletes the window's maximum height
ID 3 - Causes the window to be drawn at its remembered (or default) size immediately
ID 4 - Zeros out a window's remember size ID which defeats Remember window size

Menu Item Flag Values
Table C.8 presents values for the <menu_item_flag> parameter used with the
fdo$Menultem command.
"No action” indicates an unused flag that invokes no code and therefore causes
no changes to the menu item. Conditional statements such as “if online”
indicate that a logical test occurs and an action occurs based on whether the test
evaluates to True. If the condition evaluates to False, the opposite action
occurs. For example, the opposite of enable is disable, and the opposite of
mark is unmark (clear any mark characters).

APPENDIX D
FDO Quick Reference Lists
This appendix provides quick reference lists of all FDO commands. The lists
contain the FDO command names and the hexadecimal and decimal values for
each opcode. Subopcode hexadecimal and decimal values are provided where
applicable.
Table D.1 presents the FDO commands in alphabetical order by FDO
command name and Table D.2 presents the FDO commands in numerical
order by opcode hexadecimal value.
When you build forms, the FDO command name is entered in the FDO data
stream and converted to a numeric value by the FDO forms compiler when the
stream is compiled.

following FDO commands:

discussion of commonly used MOP codes, see the "Description of Common
MOP Codes" section below.

into the FDO data stream. When you decompile a form, the dispatch's text
value is displayed.

Descriptions of Common MOP Codes
The following commonly used MOP codes are explained in detail in the rest
of this appendix:
*» Ask DB (MOP 1)
* Ask _DB_Pop (MOP 16)
* Ask_Indirect (MOP 17)
* Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18)
* Send_Form (MOP 10)
* Send_Selected (MOP 11)
Ask_DB (MOP 1)
When you enter Ask_DB (MOP 1) as the first parameter (KSMOP_code>) of
the dispatch argument, the system attempts to display a form from the host
library. Ifthe token is 'K1', the system checks for a local copy of this library
record. If alocal copy is found, it is processed as a form.
If a local copy is not found, or the token is not 'K1', the token and the
subparameters of the third parameter (KMOP_info> ) are sent to the host
computer. The host computer then gets the library record specified in the
bytes of the subparameters and sends the form to the client computer.
Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16)
When you enter Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16) as the first parameter
(<MOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, you have the same result as
described for Ask_DB (MOP 1) except that the front-most window is popped
after the form is processed.

sections.content: The following commonly used MOP codes are explained in detail in the rest of this appendix:
sections.heading: Descriptions of Common MOP Codes

sections.content:
When you enter Ask_DB (MOP 1) as the first parameter (<MOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, the system attempts to display a form from the host library. If the token is 'K1', the system checks for a local copy of this library record. If a local copy is found, it is processed as a form.

If a local copy is not found, or the token is not 'K1', the token and the subparameters of the third parameter (<MOP_info> ) are sent to the host computer. The host computer then gets the library record specified in the bytes of the subparameters and sends the form to the client computer.
sections.heading: Ask_DB (MOP 1)

sections.content: When you enter Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16) as the first parameter (<MOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, you have the same result as described for Ask_DB (MOP 1) except that the front-most window is popped after the form is processed.
sections.heading: Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16)

Ask_Indirect (MOP 17)
When you enter Ask_Indirect (MOP 17) as the first parameter (SMOP_code>)
of the dispatch argument, a button is connected to an item on a list. When a
member presses a button that has a MOP code of 17, the dispatch looks at the
token and then the subparm1 value of <MOP_info>. The value in subparm]
for this field points to another field where other subparameter values of
<MOP_info> are stored. Once the token and the subparameter values are
established, you have the same result as described for Ask_DB (MOP 1).
Example
In the example shown in Figure E.1 below, a form is created with a list field
as field 1 and a button as field 2. The button has a MOP code of 17, with a
'K1' token, and a value of 1 for subparm1. When the member presses the
button on the form, the dispatch extracts the three subparameter values from
the list item currently selected (highlighted on the user's screen). The dispatch
then uses the token from the button field and the three subparameter values
from the list field, and executes a Ask_DB (MOP 1).
The button field's dispatch parameters appear as shown in Figure E.1:
<MOP_info>
Figure E.1 — Ask_Indirect Example
<MOP_code> | (<token><s_code>)
The six bytes of the dispatch argument are explained as follows:
Byte 1 MOP code 17 causes byte 4 of the button (field 2)
subparm] to be checked.
Byte 2/3 Token 'K1' checks for a local copy of the library
record. The s_code of 0 allows action in either the
paid or free area.
Byte 4 A value of 1 points to subparm1, subparm2, and
subparm3 of the list (field 1).
Byte 5 A value of $00 indicates that this subparameter is not
used. The dispatch uses the three subparameter
values from the list field.

Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18)
When you enter Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18) as the first parameter (<KMOP_code>)
of the dispatch argument, you have the same result as described for
Ask_Indirect (MOP 17) except that it executes the entire dispatch from the
selected field.
Send_Form (MOP 10)
When you enter Send_Form (MOP 10) as the first parameter (KMOP_code>)
of the dispatch argument, the system sends member-entered information to the
host computer. When a member clicks on a button (or information associated
with the button) that has a Send_Form dispatch definition attached to it, the
dispatch is executed, and the form's data is sent to the host according to the
values of the dispatch parameters.
By default, a Send_Form dispatch sends the contents of all input text fields on
a form to the host. The contents of other types of fields (radio box, check box, \
group, and popup fields) are not sent unless you set the FFSSendform boolean U
(boolean 4) for the field.
When the dispatch is executed, the Send_Form dispatch first looks at
subparm1 of <MOP_info> to determine what data to send. The value of
subparm] usually determines how information for a list field is sent to the
host.
The value of subparm1 affects what values you place in subparm?2 and
subparm3 of <MOP_info>. The action of subparm1 is determined by setting
the value of bits 7, 2, 1 or 0. Bits 6, 5, 4, and 3 are not used. Numbers in
parentheses are hexadecimal values.
Table E.2 shows the values for subparm1 of the Send_Form dispatch:

sections.content:
When you enter Send_Form (MOP 10) as the first parameter (<KMOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, the system sends member-entered information to the host computer. When a member clicks on a button (or information associated with the button) that has a Send_Form dispatch definition attached to it, the dispatch is executed, and the form's data is sent to the host according to the values of the dispatch parameters.

By default, a Send_Form dispatch sends the contents of all input text fields on a form to the host. The contents of other types of fields (radio box, check box, group, and popup fields) are not sent unless you set the FFSSendform boolean U (boolean 4) for the field.

When the dispatch is executed, the Send_Form dispatch first looks at subparm1 of <MOP_info> to determine what data to send. The value of subparm1 usually determines how information for a list field is sent to the host.

The value of subparm1 affects what values you place in subparm2 and subparm3 of <MOP_info>. The action of subparm1 is determined by setting the value of bits 7, 2, 1 or 0. Bits 6, 5, 4, and 3 are not used. Numbers in parentheses are hexadecimal values.

Example
In the example shown in Figure E.2 below, a form is created with a button
field as field 1 and a list field as field 2. The button has a MOP code of 10,
with a 'S1' token, a value of $04 for subparm1, a value of $02 for subparam2,
and a value of $00 for subparam3. In this example, the dispatch sends all of
the text of the currently selected list item to the host. When the button
associated with this dispatch is clicked, the rest of the contents of the form is
also sent.

fields that you want sent to the host.

If the form contains a list field, you must place a value that determines how
the list's data is sent in subparm1 (<MOP_info>). See Table E.2
If your form has a list field and you want data sent for any field between 17
and 24, set the appropriate bits in subparm1. If a conflict arises, you may
need to change the field numbers on your form. Most forms do not have more
than 16 fields, so there usually is no conflict. If you do not have a list field or
data to send for fields between 17 and 24 on your form, enter a value of $00
for subparm1.
Often when you have a list field and a Send_Selected dispatch on a form, you
need to set up the list item's subparameters in a dispatch associated with the
list item, not on the button's dispatch. This is because the subparameters of
the button's dispatch are taken up by specifying Send_Selected fields. Once a
button's dispatch is executed, the forms engine reads the value of subparm1.
This value may direct the forms engine to go to the subparameters of a list
item's dispatch.

If your form has a list field and you want data sent for any field between 17 and 24, set the appropriate bits in subparm1. If a conflict arises, you may need to change the field numbers on your form. Most forms do not have more than 16 fields, so there usually is no conflict. If you do not have a list field or data to send for fields between 17 and 24 on your form, enter a value of $00 for subparm1.

APPENDIX F
Macintosh Window Types
Macintosh window types are specified with the <window_type> variable in
FDO commands. The <window_type> value used to create each window is
listed inside the window shown in figure F.1. A number of different window
types produce windows with the same appearance with the only exception
being that some are initially invisible. Therefore, each unique window
appearance is shown once. Table C.1 in Appendix C lists definitions for each
of the window types.

sections.content: FDOS8S8 is the Form Definition Opcode (FDO) programming language for the Macintosh®; Apple®Il, and Tandy® platforms. This language controls the appearance and functionality of online service windows (forms).
sections.heading: What is FDO88?

sections.content: This manual lists, describes, and provides examples of all commands that make up the FDOS88 language. It defines the parameters used by the FDO commands and provides sample FDO data streams. This manual also illustrates the Macintosh window types that can be specified by FDO commands.
sections.heading: About This Manual

sections.content: This manual is intended for use by experienced Macintosh programmers who want to develop additional functionality for the Macintosh platform by creating or modifying forms.
sections.heading: Who Should Use This Manual

bullet_points: Chapter 1, "Introduction," defines the FDO88 language and discusses the content of this manual.
Chapter 2, "FDO88 Description," describes the FDO88 language. It contains a technical overview of FDO88 and provides descriptions, syntax, examples, and platforms for each FDO command.
sections.content: The contents of this manual are as follows:
sections.heading: Chapter Overview

sections.content:
Appendix A, "Glossary," contains definitions of some basic terms and concepts used throughout this manual.

Appendix B, "Sample FDO Streams," presents five sample FDO data streams generated by the form_edit tool and their resulting screen displays.

Appendix C, "Reference Tables," provides tables that define the parameters used by the FDO commands.

Appendix D, "FDO Quick Reference Lists," lists all FDO commands in alphabetical and numerical order. The lists contain the FDO command names and the hexadecimal and decimal values for each opcode. Subopcode hexadecimal and decimal values are provided where applicable.

Appendix E, "Menu Operation (MOP) Codes," explains the MOP codes used in the dispatch argument of the fdo$dispch, fdo$ImdDispatch, fdo$SMenultem, fdo$SNotifDispatch, and fdo$ResetDispch commands.

Appendix F, "Macintosh Window Types," provides illustrations of Macintosh window types specified with the FDO command parameter <window_type>.
Chapter 2 provides a description of the FDO88 language for the Macintosh, Apple 11, and Tandy platforms. It contains a technical overview of FDO88 and provides definitions for all FDO commands.

bullet_points: FDO command basics
Modified FDO arguments
Byte-count FDO commands
Extended FDO commands
How FDO streams are sent
sections.content: This section discusses the following subjects:
sections.heading: Technical Overview

sections.content: Each FDO command has an alphabetic name and a numeric code (opcode). You use the alphabetic name when building forms; however, you must know the opcode if you are trying to interpret a raw data stream. A few FDO commands have a sub-opcode. These exceptions are discussed in the "Extended FDO Commands" section below. In this chapter, all FDO commands are presented using their alphabetic name. The alphabetic names of FDO commands are not case-sensitive; upper case characters are used only for ease of interpretation when a command name is made up of two or more words or parts of words.
sections.heading: FDO Command Basics

sections.content:
Several FDO commands cannot stand alone. For example, a form does not compile successfully if the fdo$Text command is present without either the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command. Another example is the fdo$SetStyle command, which does not function if the fdo$Text command is not present.

An FDO command is followed by its command line arguments (parameters). These parameters provide options in the use of the command. For example, the fdo$Field command has a <type> parameter that requires specification of the field type. You can make the field type a button, a text field, or any of the other available field types.

Two types of FDO arguments are modified for transmission in an FDO data stream — flipped integer arguments and zero-terminated text arguments.

Flipped integers are two-byte integer arguments that have their low (or second) byte transmitted first, followed by their high (or first) byte. A normal two-byte integer has its high byte and then low byte transmitted.

Zero-terminated text arguments are variable-length character string arguments with a 0 appended to them. Software interpreting an FDO data stream expects zero-terminated text arguments to end with a 0. The modified arguments may not contain a 0. Both flipped integer and zero-terminated text arguments are noted as such in the "FDO Command Definitions™ section below.
sections.heading: Modified FDO Arguments

sections.content:
Extended

Byte-count FDO commands are made by having their high-bits set. The high-bit is the eighth bit in an eight-bit byte. The eight bits are assigned positions 0 through seven. In binary notation, the high-bit, if set, adds 27 (or 128) to the value of the byte. High bits are set by adding 128 to the decimal value or $80 to the hexadecimal value of an FDO command.

Byte-count FDO commands have byte count arguments which indicate the total length of the command's parameters; base FDO commands do not. The byte-count argument is rarely seen except in cases where the raw data stream is being examined by a developer, and does not need to be entered when building a form. When you look at a raw data stream, the byte-count parameter is the second value in the FDO command statement. It appears just after the FDO command's opcode.
sections.heading: Byte-Count FDO Commands

sections.content: As the FDO language grew, available opcodes were being depleted. Extended FDO commands were created to enable a large number of new commands to
sections.heading: FDO Commands

sections.content:
fit into the 128 byte limit for opcodes. These commands comprise a whole new class of FDO commands. The format of these commands includes the use of an FDO command name, which is not unique, and a sub-opcode, which is unique, in the command syntax.
Since these commands have their high bit set, the opcodes for them fall in the range of 248 (for the first group of extended commands) through 256 (for the last group of extended commands). This means that each FDO command in the first group of extended FDO commands has an opcode of 248 followed by its own unique sub-opcode, the second group of FDO commands has an opcode of 249 followed by its own unique sub-opcode, and so on. You can to assign up to 256 extended FDO commands for each group.

FDO streams can be stored in the local client database or stored and sent from the host computer. FDO streams sent from the host computer are divided into data packets. Each data packet is marked with a token which is a two byte character indicating what kind of data the packet contains.
Packets containing FDO streams are sent with a packet identifier called an fD token that signifies that the packet contains form definition information. The fD token is placed in the first two bytes of the data portion of the packet.
sections.heading: How FDO Streams are Sent

sections.content: This section provides definitions of all FDO88 commands in alphabetical order. The FDO command definitions list the associated opcodes along with descriptions, syntax, examples, and platforms for each command. Sub-opcodes are also provided, where applicable. The hexadecimal value for each opcode and sub-opcode is shown in parentheses after the decimal value.
sections.heading: FDO Command Definitions

bullet_points: fdo$command Bold text beginning with "fdo$" denotes the FDO command's alphabetic name.
<req_parm> Angle brackets denote required FDO command parameters.
(<parm1> <parm2>) Parentheses denote a group of corollary parameters. Each parameter must occur sequentially with all other parameters shown within the parentheses.
sections.content: The following conventions are used in the FDO command definitions:
sections.heading: Conventions

sections.content:
Examples in the FDO command definitions reflect how the FDO command line appears in its decompiled format. The decompiled format shows how the command stream looks before a new form has been compiled, or after an existing form is selected from the local client database or the host computer and decompiled. If you have compiled an FDO data stream and are viewing its raw format, it will not appear like the examples provided in this chapter. Only the numeric values of FDO commands exist in a raw stream.

In the FDO command examples, some parameter values are shown in hexadecimal form while others are shown in decimal form. When building a form you can use either the decimal or hexadecimal form for any parameter value. However, once the form is compiled, the parameter values you entered when the form was created may be converted from decimal to hexadecimal or vice versa by the compiler the next time you decompile the form.

All parameters are converted to hexadecimal values as the form is compiled. In its compiled state, the FDO data stream is ready to be interpreted by the FDO engine.
sections.heading: Examples

packet that the token identifies usually contains user response(s).
The surcharge code indicates whether an action is allowed from a paid area only, from a free area only, or from either. If the member triggers a dispatch and the surcharge code in the dispatch does not match the current pay status, the client software will offer to either cancel the dispatch or to change the surcharge status before executing the dispatch.
For example, if a member is in a paid area and clicks the Customer Service button whose dispatch has a surcharge code of "Free", the client software prompts the member with "Are you certain you want to enter this free area? Any chat and gateway windows will be closed. All other windows will be hidden until you exit." Surcharge code values are as follows:

subsections.content:
3 bytes of information that is dependent upon the value of the MOP code used. <MOP_info> is divided into as three subparameters: subparm1, subparm?2, and subparm3.
These subparameter bytes are sometimes taken together as a multi-byte parameter. For example, if the MOP code "Ask_DB" is used, the first subparameter contains the library number and the second and third subparameter. Taken together, these subparameters contain the two-byte record number for the host computer library record that is being requested.

Note: The high bit is sometimes set in forms received from the host computer. In this case if the high bit were set, the MOP code value would be 129. This means MOP code values from 129 to 155 are equal to MOP code values 1 to 127 respectively.

* 'K1'is the token ID
* 0 is the surcharge code.

SA8 (subparm2) Specify the record number for the data being called by and $5F (subparm3) MOP code 1.

sections.heading: <flags> Parameter Description

sections.content: Examples of how this parameter is used for the more common field types are as follows:
sections.heading: Examples

subsections.content:
fdo$Field16 Button 0106 0230 0080 0020 $00
fdo$text 00 "Setup"
fdo$dispch AskLocalDB $0000 $00 $05 $85
The first command, fdo$Field16, creates a button field whose upper left corner is horizontally positioned 106 pixels from the left side of the screen and vertically positioned 230 pixels from the top of the screen. The button's width is 80 pixels, height is 20 pixels and no flags are set.
The second command, fdo$text, places the word "Setup" on the button that was just created.
The third command, fdo$dispch, asks the local database to display the Setup form identified by the library record number shown in the last three parameters (800, $05, and $B5).
subsections.heading: Example of Button field

subsections.content:
fdo$Switch SetNewTE 00
fdo$SetUserFont $1025
fdo$Field16 Text 0075 0112 0420 0161 $D1
fdo$Mark 00 0100
fdo$Switch FieldProtectOn 00
fdo$Switch FieldAccentsOn 00
fdo$Switch SetMainTEField 00
fdo$SetSize 24000
The first command, fdo$Switch, sets the ability to use mixed fonts in the next text field that is created. If used, the SetNewTE switch must appear before the text field is created, as in this example:
The second command, fdo$SetUserFont, sets the member font preference to the one specified by the mail tool. The mail tool's ID is $1025.
The third command, fdo$Field16, creates a text field whose upper left corner is horizontally positioned 75 pixels from the left side of the screen and vertically
subsections.heading: Example of Text field

FFSScroll (Boolean 5), which lets the field be scrollable.

sections.content: positioned 112 pixels from the top of the screen. The button's width is 420 pixels and height is 161 pixels. The value of $D1 signifies that four flags are set. The flags are:

Note: The first eight booleans can be set using the <flags> parameter of the fdo$Field16 command.
The fourth command, fdo$Mark, assigns this field an identification number of 100, which is recognized by the mail tool. The mail tool was originally associated with this form by using the fdo$SetWindowTag command near the beginning of the data stream.
The fifth command, fdo$Switch, enables the switch that marks the window as changed if a member makes a change to this field.
The sixth command, fdo$Switch, enables the switch that allows accent characters and other special characters to be used.
The seventh command, fdo$SSwitch, enables the switch that makes this field the major field on the form. A status of major affects File Save and Resize menu options.
The eighth command, fdo$SetSize, limits the number of characters that can be entered into this text field to 24,000 characters.

sections.content: The first command, fdo$Field16, creates a list field whose upper left corner is horizontally positioned 20 pixels from the left side of the screen and vertically positioned 29 pixels from the top of the screen. The list's width is 270 pixels and height is 192 pixels. The value of $90 signifies that the list is boxed (Boolean 8) and scrollable (Boolean 5).

The second command, fdo$mline, creates the first menu item for the list field just created. This menu item is a folder ($02) with the title "Online Service Products Center." The third command, fdo$dispch, asks the local database (129, which is the same as MOP code 1) to display the form identified by the 3-byte library record number shown in the last three parameters ($02, $C3, and $F9) if this menu item is selected. The fourth command, fdo$mline, creates the second menu item for the list field. This menu item is a folder ($02) with the title "Online Service Software Upgrades." The fifth command, fdo$dispch, asks the local database (129, which is the same as MOP code 1) to display the form identified by the 3-byte library record number shown in the last three parameters ($02, $24, and $42) if this menu item is selected.

$05 Moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window. For example, if the field is created at X =25, with a <FieldRuleX> of 5 and a window width of 100, and the window is widened (resized) to 200, the field is repositioned to X=50.
$06 Moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field.
$07 Moves the field's center to keep its relative position within the window (same as value $05). In addition, the field is resized in proportion to the change in size of the window.
$08 Moves the field's center to keep its position relative to the window's major field (same as value $06). In addition, the field is resized in proportion to the change in size of the major field.

sections.content:
This appendix includes 14 tables that define the different parameters used by the FDO commands. Each table contains all valid values for that parameter and a description of each value's effect if used in an FDO data stream. Each table is preceded by a brief description of its purpose and content. Where applicable, brief explanations of parameters are also provided.

Values are presented in the most common form in which they are used. In some cases, such as in Table C.2, Field Types, both the numeric and text values of the argument are shown since you can specify either value for the parameter when you build a form. For any numeric value, you can use either decimal or hexadecimal format.

subsections.content: Table C.1 presents values for the <window_type> parameter used with the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command. Values from 1 to 8 are only applicable to the Macintosh. Macintosh window types are illustrated in Appendix F, "Macintosh Window Types."
subsections.heading: Window Types

bullet_points: ModalStandard: Standard, double line border modal window.
ModelessRound: Round-edged, modeless window with close box and no resize.
Standard: Normal window with grow and zoom boxes.
ModalShadow: Modal window with drop shadow.
ModalSingleBorder: Modal window with single line border.
Modeless window with no close box.
sections.heading: Table C.1 Window Types

sections.content: FFTSmallPopup 27 Popup Popup menu field that uses 9 point Geneva font.

When a field is specified using the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command, there are 37 booleans associated with it. The state of the boolean (True/on or False/off) can be set in two different ways. For this reason, this table is split into two parts.

Table C.3 presents the first eight booleans, which are usually set by the <flags> parameter of the fdo$MacIcon, fdo$Field, or fdo$Field16 command (but can be set using the fdo$Switch command).

The first eight booleans are multiplexed, which means they have a different meaning depending on the field type (text, button, list, etc.). The remaining booleans are presented in Table C.4.

When you build a form, you set the first eight booleans by using their hexadecimal value. The total value determines which of the seven flag(s) are set. For example, a value of $90 means that Boolean 5, which has a hexadecimal value of $10, and Boolean 8, which has a hexadecimal value of $80, are set.
sections.heading: Multiplexed Field Booleans

subsections.content: FFSSelSensitive. If True (134), causes client software to execute the list item's dispatch when the member changes the currently selected list item. Used in the System Preferences window. False value is 174.
subsections.heading: Boolean 34

subsections.content: RESERVED FOR PCAO - DO NOT USE! If True (134), causes client software to execute a list item's dispatch when the member changes the currently selected list item. Used in the System Preferences window. False value is 174.
subsections.heading: Boolean 35

subsections.content: FFSEnterKey Set via switch 239 (SetEnterKey).
subsections.heading: Boolean 36

sections.content:
The fdo$Switch command can be used with either the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command or the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command. Table C.5 presents the values used for the <switch_number> parameter of the fdo$Switch command.

Switch values 101 through 180 affect the field booleans described in Table C.3 and C.4. Switch values 101 through 140 turn on field booleans 1 to 40, respectively; switch values 141 through 180 turn off field booleans 1 to 40, respectively.

Although all switch values 101 through 180 change the state of a corresponding field boolean, not all of these switch values are useful. For example, switches 110 and 150 are not meaningful because they would change the state of a text field's FFSHaveTE boolean (Boolean 10) which is a read-only boolean.

When you build a form, use the numeric or text value. When a form is decompiled using the FDO forms compiler, the numeric value for this parameter is displayed.

For more information, see the definition of the fdo$Switch command in Chapter 2.

Most often the fdo$Switch command is placed immediately after the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command in the FDO data stream; however there are a few cases where it must be placed before.
sections.heading: Field Switch Values

sections.content:
Switch Text Value Description
SetEnterKeyField Maps the ENTER (or COMMAND-RETURN) key(s) to the specified field. If the member presses ENTER (or COMMAND-RETURN), the AOL software acts as though the specified field was clicked. This switch is used by the Send Now button on the Compose Mail form and the Send button on the Instant Message form.

Table C.6 lists the values used by the <switch_number> parameter of the fdo$Switch command. They are used to make changes to the current window.
When you build a form, use these values for the fdo$Switch command when it follows the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command.
Some switches have different numeric and text values for their true and false states. For these types of switches, both the numerical and text values are provided, with the true setting appearing first. The associated switch description is for the true setting (see Switch 1 and 2).
When you build a form, use the numeric value of the switch. When a form is decompiled using the FDO forms compiler, only the numeric value for this parameter is displayed.
Table C.5 presents other switch values, but those are used by the fdo$Switch command to make changes to the current field in a form.
sections.heading: Window Switch Values

sections.content: Note: In most cases, the fdo$Switch command is placed immediately after the fdo$Window or fdo$Window16 command in the FDO data stream; however there are some exceptions.

This appendix provides quick reference lists of all FDO commands. The lists contain the FDO command names and the hexadecimal and decimal values for each opcode. Subopcode hexadecimal and decimal values are provided where applicable.

Table D.1 presents the FDO commands in alphabetical order by FDO command name and Table D.2 presents the FDO commands in numerical order by opcode hexadecimal value.

When you build forms, the FDO command name is entered in the FDO data stream and converted to a numeric value by the FDO forms compiler when the stream is compiled.

sections.content: Appendix E explains the MOP codes used in the dispatch argument of the following FDO commands:
sections.heading: FDO Commands Using MOP Codes

sections.content:
Table E.1 lists MOP code values and brief descriptions. For a more detailed discussion of commonly used MOP codes, see the "Description of Common MOP Codes" section below.

When building a form, enter either the numeric or text value of the MOP code into the FDO data stream. When you decompile a form, the dispatch's text value is displayed.
sections.heading: MOP Code Table

bullet_points: Ask_DB (MOP 1)
Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16)
Ask_Indirect (MOP 17)
Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18)
Send_Form (MOP 10)
Send_Selected (MOP 11)
sections.content: The following commonly used MOP codes are explained in detail in the rest of this appendix:
sections.heading: Descriptions of Common MOP Codes

sections.content:
When you enter Ask_DB (MOP 1) as the first parameter (<MOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, the system attempts to display a form from the host library. If the token is 'K1', the system checks for a local copy of this library record. If a local copy is found, it is processed as a form.

If a local copy is not found, or the token is not 'K1', the token and the subparameters of the third parameter (<MOP_info> ) are sent to the host computer. The host computer then gets the library record specified in the bytes of the subparameters and sends the form to the client computer.
sections.heading: Ask_DB (MOP 1)

sections.content: When you enter Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16) as the first parameter (<MOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, you have the same result as described for Ask_DB (MOP 1) except that the front-most window is popped after the form is processed.
sections.heading: Ask_DB_Pop (MOP 16)

sections.content: When you enter Ask_Indirect (MOP 17) as the first parameter (SMOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, a button is connected to an item on a list. When a member presses a button that has a MOP code of 17, the dispatch looks at the token and then the subparm1 value of <MOP_info>. The value in subparm] for this field points to another field where other subparameter values of <MOP_info> are stored. Once the token and the subparameter values are established, you have the same result as described for Ask_DB (MOP 1).
sections.heading: Ask_Indirect (MOP 17)

bullet_points: Byte 1 MOP code 17 causes byte 4 of the button (field 2) subparm] to be checked.
Byte 2/3 Token 'K1' checks for a local copy of the library record. The s_code of 0 allows action in either the paid or free area.
Byte 4 A value of 1 points to subparm1, subparm2, and subparm3 of the list (field 1).
Byte 5 A value of $00 indicates that this subparameter is not used. The dispatch uses the three subparameter values from the list field.
subsections.content:
In the example shown in Figure E.1 below, a form is created with a list field as field 1 and a button as field 2. The button has a MOP code of 17, with a 'K1' token, and a value of 1 for subparm1. When the member presses the button on the form, the dispatch extracts the three subparameter values from the list item currently selected (highlighted on the user's screen). The dispatch then uses the token from the button field and the three subparameter values from the list field, and executes a Ask_DB (MOP 1).

The button field's dispatch parameters appear as shown in Figure E.1:
<MOP_info>
Figure E.1 — Ask_Indirect Example
<MOP_code> | (<token><s_code>)
The six bytes of the dispatch argument are explained as follows:
subsections.heading: Example

sections.content:
used. The dispatch uses the three subparameter values from the list field.
Byte 6 A value of $00 indicates that this subparameter is not used.

When you enter Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18) as the first parameter (<KMOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, you have the same result as described for Ask_Indirect (MOP 17) except that it executes the entire dispatch from the selected field.
sections.heading: Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18)

sections.content:
When you enter Send_Form (MOP 10) as the first parameter (<KMOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, the system sends member-entered information to the host computer. When a member clicks on a button (or information associated with the button) that has a Send_Form dispatch definition attached to it, the dispatch is executed, and the form's data is sent to the host according to the values of the dispatch parameters.

By default, a Send_Form dispatch sends the contents of all input text fields on a form to the host. The contents of other types of fields (radio box, check box, group, and popup fields) are not sent unless you set the FFSSendform boolean U (boolean 4) for the field.

When the dispatch is executed, the Send_Form dispatch first looks at subparm1 of <MOP_info> to determine what data to send. The value of subparm1 usually determines how information for a list field is sent to the host.

The value of subparm1 affects what values you place in subparm2 and subparm3 of <MOP_info>. The action of subparm1 is determined by setting the value of bits 7, 2, 1 or 0. Bits 6, 5, 4, and 3 are not used. Numbers in parentheses are hexadecimal values.

Table E.2 shows the values for subparm1 of the Send_Form dispatch:
sections.heading: Send_Form (MOP 10)

sections.content:
The six bytes of the dispatch argument are explained as follows:
Byte 1 MOP code 10 issues a Send_Form dispatch.
Byte 2/3 Token 'S1' identifies the host process to which this data packet is sent. The s_code of 0 allows action in either the paid or free area.
Byte 4 A value of $04 indicates that Bit 2 is set. This sends text of the currently selected list item is in accordance with the value in subparm2 and subparm3.
Byte 5 A value of $02 causes the client computer to start sending the text with the first character.
Byte 6 A value of $00 causes the client computer to send all of the text.

When you enter Send_Selected (MOP 11 ) as the first parameter (<MOP_code>) of the dispatch argument, you have the same result as described for Send_Form (MOP 10) except that it allows you to specify the fields that you want sent to the host.
Table E.3 shows which bits of the last parameter (<Mop_info>) of the dispatch argument to set to send specific fields to the host:
sections.heading: Send_Selected (MOP 11)

bullet_points: Subparm1 To send fields 17 through 24 to the host, set bits 0 through 7, respectively. For example, to send field number 17 to the host, set bit 0.
Subparm?2 To send fields 9 through 16 to the host, set bits 0 through 7, respectively. For example, to send field number 10 to the host, set bit 1.
subsections.content:
24 22 22 20 1 16 15 1 9 1 8
Subparml Subparm2 Subparm3
The three subparameters of the <Mop_info> parameter are explained as follows:
subsections.heading: Table E.3 Send_Selected Fields

sections.content:
Subparm3 To send fields 1 through 8 to the host, set bits 0 through 7, respectively. For example, to send field number 4 to the host, set bit 3.

If the form contains a list field, you must place a value that determines how the list's data is sent in subparm1 (<MOP_info>). See Table E.2

If your form has a list field and you want data sent for any field between 17 and 24, set the appropriate bits in subparm1. If a conflict arises, you may need to change the field numbers on your form. Most forms do not have more than 16 fields, so there usually is no conflict. If you do not have a list field or data to send for fields between 17 and 24 on your form, enter a value of $00 for subparm1.

Often when you have a list field and a Send_Selected dispatch on a form, you need to set up the list item's subparameters in a dispatch associated with the list item, not on the button's dispatch. This is because the subparameters of the button's dispatch are taken up by specifying Send_Selected fields. Once a button's dispatch is executed, the forms engine reads the value of subparm1. This value may direct the forms engine to go to the subparameters of a list item's dispatch.

bullet_points: Byte 1 MOP code 11 issues a Send_Selected dispatch.
Byte 2/3 Token 'S1' identifies the host process to which this data packet is sent. The s_code of 0 allows action in either the paid or free area.
Byte 4 A value of $02 indicates that Bit 1 is set for the list field type. This directs the forms engine to get the

Byte 5 A value of $06 indicates that bit 1 ($02) and bit 2 ($04) are set. This sends data from fields 10 and 11 to the host computer.
Byte 6 A value of $01 indicates that bit 1 ($01) is set. This sends data from field 1 to the host computer.

The six bytes of the dispatch argument are explained as follows:
Byte 1 MOP code 11 issues a Send_Selected dispatch.
Byte 2/3 Token 'S1' identifies the host process to which this data packet is sent. The s_code of 0 allows action in either the paid or free area.
Byte 4 A value of $05 indicates that Bit 2 ($04) is set for the list field type and bit 0 ($01) is set for field 17.
Byte 5 A value of $00 indicates that there are no other specified Send_Selected fields on the form.
Byte 6 A value of $00 indicates that there are no other specified Send_Selected fields on the form.

Note: There are rarely 17 fields on a form so the example is an unlikely dispatch. However, it demonstrates the use of subparm1 when bits must be set for both a list field type and a non-list field type.

The <window_type> value used to create each window is listed inside the window shown in figure F.1. A number of different window types produce windows with the same appearance with the only exception being that some are initially invisible. Therefore, each unique window appearance is shown once. Table C.1 in Appendix C lists definitions for each of the window types.

commands.description: fdo$248_Extended $23 (EndListItem) ends the definition of a list box item. fdo$EndDef also ends the definition of the current list box item. fdo$StartListItem ends the definition of a list box item before it starts a new list box item.
commands.name: fdo$248_Extended

commands.description:
fdo$248_Extended $21 (TypedTitle) is used in place of fdo$Title when typed data is sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$248_Extended $21 (TypedTitle) changes the title of the current window to the specified title string.
Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. For more information, see the fdo$Title definition.
The text sent by fdo$248_Extended $21 (TypedTitle) can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.
commands.example_explanation: This example changes the title of the current window to the ASCII data (type $0000) string "Setup"( $53 $65 $74 $75 $70 ).
