commands.description:
fdo$STypedMakeMenu is used in place of fdo$MakeMenu when typed data is being sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$TypedMakeMenu creates a Macintosh hierarchical menu that has specific cluster and ID values assigned to it. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. For more information, see the fdo$TypedMakeMenu definition.

Note: To use the fdo$STypedMakeMenu command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $1F form of the command in your code.

The text sent by fdo$TypedMakeMenu can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.
commands.name: fdo$TypedMakeMenu
commands.notes: To use the fdo$STypedMakeMenu command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $1F form of the command in your code.

parameters.description: Identifies the group of windows or menus where you want the menu created by this command to be placed. Menu cluster values are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <menu_cluster>

parameters.description: Defines a unique numeric menu identifier for this menu. Each menu in an FDO stream must be assigned a unique menu ID number. Values are 0 to 5000.
parameters.name: <menu_id>

parameters.description: Not supported. Leave set to 0.
parameters.name: <owner>

values.description: Default value as the parameter is not supported

parameters.description: Specifies the ID number for the Macintosh menu to be used in a hierarchical America Online Confidential 2-144 © America Online, Inc. 1993-94
parameters.name: <menu_id_internal>

commands.description:
fdo$TypedMenuItem is used in place of fdo$MenuItem when typed data is sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$TypedMenuItem adds a menu item to the menu created with fdo$TypedMakeMenu. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. For more information, see the fdo$TypedMakeMenu definition.

Note: To use the fdo$TypedMenuItem command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $20 form of the command in your code.

The text sent by fdo$TypedMenuItem can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.
commands.name: fdo$TypedMenuItem
commands.notes: To use the fdo$TypedMenuItem command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $20 form of the command in your code.

parameters.description: Identifies a particular group of windows or menus. This parameter specifies the cluster of the menu to which the new menu item is added. Menu cluster values are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <menu_cluster>

parameters.description: Used to associate a specific menu to the menu item being added. Each menu in an FDO stream must be assigned a unique number. Values are 0 to 5000.
parameters.name: <menu_id>

parameters.description: Defines the item's position within the menu.
parameters.name: <position>

commands.description: Command to add a single entry to an existing menu created by fdo$TypedMakeMenu.
commands.example_explanation: In this example, the fdo$TypedMenuItem command adds a single entry to the new menu created by the fdo$TypedMakeMenu command.
commands.name: fdo$TypedMenuItem

commands.description:
fdo$TypedTitle is used in place of fdo$Title when typed data is sent rather than 7-bit ASCII text. fdo$TypedTitle changes the title of the current window to the specified title string. Only ASCII, Unicode, and compressed Unicode types are meaningful to this command. For more information, see the fdo$Title definition.

Note: To use the fdo$TypedTitle command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $21 form of the command in your code.

The text sent by fdo$TypedTitle can be in any defined language or script, but the Macintosh is restricted to using the system script chosen by the member. For example, if the text is sent in Hebrew and the member has chosen English as the Macintosh system script, the text appears in the English equivalents of the keys used to type the Hebrew text.
commands.name: fdo$TypedTitle
commands.notes: To use the fdo$TypedTitle command with the form_edit tool, you must enter the fdo$248_Extended $21 form of the command in your code.

parameters.description: Parameter that identifies the type.
parameters.name: <type>

values.description: ASCII
Unicode
Compressed Unicode

parameters.description: Byte stream containing the data for the window title. The end of the stream is determined from the length of the FDO command.

commands.description:
fdo$UseDisplay checks to see if a window with the specified cluster and ID exists. If so, that window is brought to the front and the processing of the current FDO stream stops. If the window does not exist, no action is taken, and the remainder of the FDO stream is processed.
This command is useful when you want one copy of a given form to be displayed, such as the help form or the welcome form. You must first define a cluster and ID using fdo$SetWindowTag. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition.
commands.example_explanation: This example brings window ID number 100 of the Chat cluster (S4343) to the front.
commands.name: fdo$UseDisplay

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the window cluster of the window that is brought to the front. It is assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition. Window_cluster values are listed in Table C.13 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <window_cluster>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the window that is brought to the front. It is a unique, numeric identifier assigned by the fdo$SetWindowTag command. For more information, see the fdo$SetWindowTag definition.
parameters.name: <window_id>

commands.description:
fdo$UseRelativeRect positions the next field that is received relative to the specified field.
fdo$field is used to position fields absolutely.
commands.example_explanation: This example positions the upper left hand corner of the next field that is received 2304 pixels horizontally and 2 pixels vertically relative to field number 2.
commands.name: fdo$UseRelativeRect

parameters.description: Designates the field to which the next field received is relatively placed. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <field_number>

parameters.description: Specifies the horizontal location in pixels of the upper left hand corner of the next field received. This location is relative to the horizontal location of the field specified in <field_number>. A value of 0 arranges fields in the same row.
parameters.name: <relative_x>

values.description: Arranges fields in the same row

parameters.description: Specifies the vertical location in pixels of the upper left hand corner of the next field received. This location is relative to the vertical location of the field specified in <field_number>. A value of 0 arranges fields in the same column.
parameters.name: <relative_y>

values.description: Arranges fields in the same column

commands.description: fdo$Value deletes a menu.
commands.example_explanation: This example deletes menu ID number 100 from menu cluster $4141.
commands.name: fdo$Value

parameters.description: Deletes the menu. The only valid value is 1.
parameters.name: <command_code>

values.description: Valid value to delete the menu

parameters.description: Not supported. Leave set to 0.
parameters.name: <dont_care>

values.description: Default value as the parameter is not supported

parameters.description: Parameter that specifies the menu cluster of the menu to delete.
parameters.name: <menu_cluster>

parameters.description: Parameter that designates the menu ID of the menu to delete.
parameters.name: <menu_id>

commands.description: . fdo$window creates a window on the client computer screen using pixel coordinates. This command follows fdo$start. If you do not specify a title, the window is titled "UNTITLED." Windows can also be defined with character coordinates using fdo$window16.
commands.name: fdo$window

parameters.description: Defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the window in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <column>

values.description: Centers the window horizontally and vertically.
Causes this parameter to be ignored.

parameters.description: Defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the window in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <row>

values.description: Causes <column> to be ignored and each new window to be staggered below and to the right of the previous window.
Causes the system to reset window staggering and place this window in the upper left of the screen.

parameters.description: Defines the width of the window in character coordinates. A character coordinate is the height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide).
parameters.name: <width>

commands.description: . fdo$Window16 creates a window using pixel coordinates. Windows can also be defined with character coordinates using fdo$window.
commands.name: fdo$Window16

parameters.description: Defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the window in pixel coordinates.
parameters.name: <column>

values.description: Centers the window horizontally and vertically.
Causes this parameter to be ignored.

parameters.description: Defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the window in pixel coordinates.
parameters.name: <row>

values.description: Causes <column> to be ignored and each new window to be staggered below and to the right of the previous window.
Causes the system to reset window staggering and place this window in the upper left of the screen.

parameters.description: Defines the width of the window in pixel coordinates. This is a flipped integer argument.
parameters.name: <width>

commands.description: fdo$field defines the position and size of a field on a form using character coordinates. Two types of coordinates are used for placing and sizing fields on a form: character, the width or height of a character cell (16 pixels high by 6 pixels wide). pixel, the size of a screen pixel. fdo$field uses character coordinates and fdo$Field16 uses pixel coordinates. Note: Each field defined, except for text fields, must have a dispatch type assigned to it. For more information, see the fdo$dispch definition.
commands.name: fdo$field
commands.notes: Each field defined, except for text fields, must have a dispatch type assigned to it.

parameters.description: defines the field type. Field type values are listed in Table C.2 in Appendix C.
parameters.name: <type>

values.description: Valid range for field type

parameters.description: defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the field by the number of character coordinates from the left side of the screen.
parameters.name: <column>

values.description: Valid range for column position

parameters.description: defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the field by the number of character coordinates from the top of the screen.
parameters.name: <row>

values.description: Valid range for row position

parameters.description: defines the width of the field by the number of character coordinates.
parameters.name: <width>

values.description: Valid range for field width

parameters.description: defines the height of the field by the number of character coordinates.
parameters.name: <height>

values.description: Valid range for field height

parameters.description: Included in syntax line; specific details not provided in this excerpt.
parameters.name: <flags>

commands.description: fdo$cancel inserts a Cancel button at the specified location. This command defines the button appearance and the action that occurs when the button is selected. When a member presses the Cancel button, the form that contains the button is popped.
commands.example_explanation: This example inserts a Cancel button at the location specified by the character coordinates 1 in the <column> field and 50 in the <row> field.
commands.name: fdo$cancel

parameters.description: defines the horizontal position of the upper left corner of the Cancel button in character coordinates. Values are 0 to 255.
parameters.name: <column>

parameters.description: defines the vertical position of the upper left corner of the Cancel button in character coordinates. Values are 0 to 255.
parameters.name: <row>

commands.description:
fdo$FillText fills a text field, list field, sketchpad® field, tool image field, bitmap field, or data field with the contents of a Macintosh resource. The type of resource used depends on the field type.

Note: To use fdo$FillText, a text field must have the FFSHaveTE flag set with the fdo$Switch command. For more information, see the fdo$Switch definition.
commands.name: fdo$FillText
commands.notes: To use fdo$FillText for text fields, the FFSHaveTE flag must be set via the fdo$Switch command.

parameters.description: Designates the field identifier number in which the contents of a Macintosh resource is placed. Each field has an associated sequential field identifier number (for example, the first field defined is field 1, the second field is field 2, etc.). Field identifier numbers are used by the host computer to reference fields.
parameters.name: <field_number>

parameters.description: Designates the resource ID number of the Macintosh resource. The resource type used depends on the target field's type.
parameters.name: <resource_ID>

values.description: 'TEXT', and optionally 'styl' (if the field has the FFSUseFonts flag set to True). To use the fdo$FillText command for text fields, the FFSHaveTE flag must be set.
'TEXT'
'Sket'
Resource type specified in the field's FieldGVal

commands.description: fdo$pop pops a specified number of forms from the client computer window. This command is not normally used within Macintosh FDO streams. If used on the Macintosh, it is used only on modal windows, since the member can re-order modeless windows at any time.
commands.example_explanation: This example pops 1 form from the client computer window.
commands.name: fdo$pop

parameters.description: parameter that designates the number of forms to be popped.
parameters.name: <n_to_pop>

commands.description:
fdo$RememberSizeIDLong is identical to fdo$RememberSizeID, except that it accepts a long integer resize ID. This command must only be used to assign global ID as a resize ID. Global IDs are in a range that are different than the resize IDs derived from cluster and ID.

Windows that do not have a resize ID specified with fdo$RememberSizelD use the window's cluster and ID as a 32-bit resize ID. For more information, see the fdo$RememberSizeID definition.
commands.example_explanation: This example establishes the ID to be used for remembering the window's size as 10.
commands.name: fdo$RememberSizeIDLong

parameters.description: parameter that designates the ID used for remembering the window's size.
parameters.name: <resize_ID>

commands.description:
fdo$reset resets the application. For the Apple II and the Tandy, all menus and open forms are cleared from the client computer screen.
For the Macintosh, the application is reset to its default state (i.e., logging on or off, not uploading or downloading, no active hotkeys). This is often used before a member enters a chat room.
commands.example_explanation: This example specifies a reset resulting from a chat room entry.
commands.name: fdo$reset

parameters.description: identifies the circumstances of the reset. Values are follows:
parameters.name: <reset_type>

values.description: Specifies a reset resulting from a chat room entry.
Specifies a reset not resulting from a chat room entry.

glossary.definition: FDO command that does not have its high bit set. Base FDO commands have no byte count arguments associated with them. (See byte-count FDO.)
FDO command argument that is one byte in length.
Total length of the data accompanying the FDO byte-count argument.
FDO command that has its high bit set. A byte-count FDO command has a byte count argument associated with it that indicates the number of bytes of data associated with the command.
Alphanumeric text string that is terminated with a zero.

Client computer screen coordinates in which the screen is divided into a finite number of columns and rows. Using character coordinates, items can be positioned on the screen in increments of the size of one column or row. On the Apple II text display screen, the size of columns and rows is based on the size of one character.
Keystroke sequence (pressing the COMMAND key and the PERIOD key simultaneously) used to abort/cancel the current action.
Window into which data is being sent.
Six-byte set of parameters designating an action to be performed by the host or client computer.
Grouping of related windows and/or menus used by the Stratus to track user location.
Acronymn for Form Definition Opcode language, which is made up of commands that control the appearance or functionality of an online service form/window.
Smallest functional unit on a form. Field types include buttons, input fields, pictures, icons, lists, and text fields. See Table C.2 in Appendix C for a complete list of fields.

Sequential field identifier assigned to each field in the order it is defined (i.e., the first field defined is field 1, the second field defined is field 2). The field number is specified in FDO command syntax statements by the <field_number > argument.
Icon that is selectable and has a centered title underneath it.

FDO parameter that is two bytes in length. The integer contains a high byte and a low byte. They are normally transmitted high byte to low byte. Machines with a 6502 microprocessor (i.e., Apple II, Commodore C64) interpret
Individual window display. Forms may contain any number of fields and field types.

FDO command parameter that requires two bytes of storage. The integer's value, divided by 256, is stored in the high byte of the integer's value. The remainder of this division is stored in the low byte.
Many FDO command definitions contain flipped integer arguments.
glossary.term: integer data in a flipped manner, low byte to high byte

glossary.definition: Window that is added to the menu option titled "Window" when closed.
Macintosh window to which the user must respond. When a modal window is opened, the menu bar is inactive, and the user cannot activate any other windows.
Macintosh window scheme in which the user may at any time activate another window or menu bar option.
Acronym for Menu Operation Code, which occupies the first two bytes of a dispatch. The MOP defines what action occurs when the user selects the field associated with the dispatch.
Icon with a mask. A mask is the area that is highlighted when the multi-icon is highlighted.
Online service member currently using one of the telecommunications networks that the online service supports.
Associated with where the window is placed on the computer monitor. One increment equals one pixel. One pixel equals 1/72".
Action of clearing a form/window from the screen of the client computer.
Block of data identified by a four-character resource type and an integer resource ID. Resources are stored on Macintosh disks.
Identification number assigned to a Macintosh resource. The resource ID is then used to call the object during a software routine. (See also resource.)
Collection of any number of individual forms comprising an online dialog that presents information or asks questions.
Number zero cannot be included in zero-terminated text strings.
Played in full before the system resumes its current operation. Synchronous sounds must be kept short so that software functionality is not slowed.
Built-in Macintosh text editor.
Currently active window. The top-most window has an active title bar and is normally the window last selected by the user.

Variable-length FDO command parameter. Its length is determined by the amount of information being sent. A string (S*) parameter, which is used on fields where a user can enter varying amounts of data, is an example of a variable-length parameter.
Area on the client computer screen that defines the bounds of the form. Fields are defined and placed within the

Pre-defined window definition known as a window resource. Window resources are created when a desired window cannot be created using the FDO language. The programmer defines a window and assigns it a resource ID.
whose last character is zero.
Box located on the upper right-hand side of some windows which, when clicked on, alternately increases and decreases the window's size.

rows.col3: Standard, modeless window that is invisible when opened and hidden when the member clicks on the close box.
Standard, modeless window that is hidden, but not closed when the member clicks on the close box.
Normal, modeless window that sets the window's kind to wMail.
Normal, modeless window that sets the window's kind to wMessage.
Modal window that can be moved and has rounded corners and a title bar. Used by file transfer gauge.
Normal, modeless window with no resize or ZOOm.
Normal, modeless window with no resize, zoom, or close.
Standard, modeless window that is hidden, but not closed when the member clicks on the. close box with no resize or zoom.
Standard, modeless window that is invisible when opened and hidden when the member clicks on the close box with no resize or Zoom.
Normal, modeless window that sets the window's kind to wMail with no resize or Zoom.
Normal, modeless window that sets the window's kind to wMessage with no resize Or zoom.
Modeless window that is invisible, with resize and zoom but, unlike Type 7, is closed when the member clicks on the close box.

reference_tables.notes: Table C.2 presents available values for the <type> parameter used with the fdo$Field or fdo$Field16 command. The Field Type, noted on the table, is only used by the FDO Language source code. When building a form, use either the numeric value or the text value.

rows.col3: Text field.
Button field.
List box field.
Menu item field
Chat status line field
Apple 11 style radio button group field.
Icon field.
Check box field.
Radio box field.
Apple style radio button group field.
File transfer gauge field
Field used for a selectable area on the screen that has an associated action.
Popup menu field that uses 12 point Chicago font.
Field used to hold file pathnames.
SketchImage
rows.col4: Field used for vector drawing commands.

rows.col3: Holder
rows.col4: Place-holding field used to store data not visible to the member.

rows.col3: Toolkit
rows.col4: Field maintained and drawn by an online service tool.

rows.col3: Bitmap
rows.col4: Field that contains bitmap data of the Apple data type BitMap. The contents of a Bitmap field is set with fdo$SetData commands or via fdo$FillText which fills the bitmap field with the contents of a 'Btmp' resource.

rows.col3: PopButton
rows.col4: Special field, like a popup menu, that does not have a title and can contain a separate dispatch for each selected item.

rows.col3: ScrollToolkit
rows.col4: Field that is a variation of the Toolkit field where the main application maintains horizontal and/or vertical scroll-bars and notifies the tool when the member scrolls the field.

rows.col3: ToolScrollImage
rows.col4: Field that is a variation of the ToolImage field, where the main application maintains horizontal and/or vertical scrollbars and notifies the tool when the member scrolls the field.

reference_tables.notes: Table C.3 lists Boolean flags categorized under Boolean 1 and Boolean 2 groups. Hex values are provided for specific primary flags ($01, $02, $08 |).

rows.Description: Creates small buttons for button fields.
Highlights the picture or icon with a large rectangle with rounded corners.

Indicates that the member must enter data into an editable text field before a SendForm dispatch is invoked. This flag is used on the Member Name and Message fields in the Compose Instant Message form to require that the member enter a value for both fields before clicking on the Send button.

Forces the system to use Text Edit to create a non-editable text field. This enables different text justifications (e.g., right aligned, centered), lets members select and copy text from the field, and makes the use of fdo$FillText possible.
Requests that multi-icon fields be copied onto a form without its mask.
Sends text field and its contents to the host if the member presses return.
Makes all fields but text fields initially invisible to the member.

Displays an asterisk on the client computer screen for each character typed in an editable, non-scrollable text field. Identifies a button field as the default button. Causes a double line around the button and makes pressing a carriage return equivalent to selecting the button.
Keeps scrolling always on for editable, scrollable text fields. Used on chat screens.

rows.col2: Requests that the value of a RadioBox, CheckBox, or Popup field be sent to the host. Normally, these field values are not sent to the host.
Requires the user to double click to select an icon, multi-icon, or picture field.
Uses the resource name to title the graphic of an icon, picture, or titled multi-icon field. FEFSResName is only meaningful if FFSTitled is set.
Makes a picture, icon, multi-icon, or button field not selectable.
Makes a text field not editable, but lets it function as an input field.
Makes a picture or icon field act like a Finder icon. It is selectable and has a centered title underneath it and centers it overits X and Y position. See the discussion of fdo$MultiIcon.

Flips a radio box or check box field's default value to its other value. If the radio box or check box is on, it is turned off and vice versa.
Note that for list fields, FFSFlip sets up the field so that pressing RETURN has the same result as double-clicking on the selected list item. Use of this flag is not recommended since the member has no visual indication of what the RETURN key will do. A better user interface is to add a default button to the form and put a Xeq_Indirect (MOP 18) on the button that points to the list field. This has the same effect as causing the RETURN key to invoke the selected list item and gives the member a clear indication of what will happen.

reference_tables.notes:
Table C.3 presents Multiplexed Field Booleans (Continued). Boolean 8 Encloses a text, list, picture, gauge, icon, multi-icon, menu item, toolkit, blank, file, or doodlefield with a box.

Table C.4 presents the remaining booleans, which are set using the <switch_number> parameter of the fdo$Switch command. When you build a form, set these booleans using the numeric switch value noted on the table. Two switch values are shown for each boolean; one sets the switch to True and the other to False.

Table C.5, which presents the field-related <switch_number> values used by the fdo$Switch command, also reflects these values.

rows.col5: Gives a gauge field a percentage label.

rows.col3: 109 (True)
rows.col5: If True, makes any field visible.

rows.col3: 111 (True)
rows.col5: If True, highlights any non-text field.

rows.col3: 112 (True)
rows.col5: If True, allows text fields and list boxes to have text in more than one script (e.g., Kanji and Roman).

rows.col3: 114 (True)
rows.col5: If True, makes a text field clickable. If a dispatch is installed into a text field using fdo$ResetDispch, the field acts like a button.

reference_tables.table_title: Multiplexed Field Booleans (Continued) and Remaining Field Booleans

rows.col3: 115 (True) If True, allows extended ASCII characters, i.e., accented characters and special symbols, to be used in a text field.
_ Not used.
117 (True) If True, allows text fields to use mixed fonts, styles, and sizes.
N/A Not used.
Used internally only.
120 (True) If True, marks the entire form as modified if a change is made to a member-changable field (i.e., editable text field, radio button, check box, popup, file field).
121 (True) If True, scrollable incoming text is not scrolled regardless of member preference.
N/A Not used.

123 (True) If True, lets picture and icon fields, that are otherwise disabled, be clickable if the member holds down the COMMAND, OPTION, and CAPS LOCK keys. Hidden features, such as credit screens, can be added with this feature.
N/A Not used.
125 (True) If True, ensures that the scroll bar stays at the bottom of the text when a text field is resized. Used for chat buffer in chat windows.

reference_tables.notes: Table C.4 is a continuation of the Field Booleans reference table. The 'Switch Value' column indicates the numeric values corresponding to True and False states for each boolean flag.

rows.col3: If True, the field is drawn dimmed. Note that button fields are automatically drawn dimmed if they are disabled.
(No specific description text provided in this segment)
If True, triggers the button, check box, radio button, icon, multi-icon fields when the member presses RETURN. For titled Pic, Icon, and MultiIcon fields, this causes the field's title to be boxed.
If True, makes the field tabable so that certain fields default to tabable when the member presses the TAB key, this field is selected.
If True, the member can make this field the active field by clicking on it. By default, input text fields and list fields are selectable.

If True (SetMasterDisplay), causes the window to display the cluster's master window. Once the window is closed, all other windows and associated menus of the cluster are automatically closed.
If True, (SetHideDisplay) allows a window to be closed but not deleted. It is available as an option under the Window menu once closed. This concept is known as making a window invisible.

If True (KeyPassDisplay), enables the window's Transparent flag. When a transparent window is front-most and the member presses a key, the transparent window is behind the second window, and the keystroke is sent to the second window. Incoming Instant Messages, Flashsession Status, and Network News have this flag enabled.

Allows mixed fonts and styles for a text field when executed on a window before the text field is created. When using SetNewTE switch, you must ensure it appears before the text field that it affects is created. This switch sets the text field's FESUseFonts flag.

Sets the global title string to the next available untitled string (e.g., "Untitled 1"). The global title string is a variable within the software that contains the name of the window to be used when a member opens an untitled window.
Sets the current prompt to the schedule string. For example: "FlashSession session scheduled for Thurs, May 13, 1993 at 6:15:00 P".
Sets a flag indicating that the Welcome screen is completed and sets the Welcome screen's mailbox icon to the appropriate state. This switch is used in the Welcome screen.

Places network setup information such as baud rates, phone numbers, network, etc., into the appropriately marked fields when executed on a window where the cluster is SetupCluster. This switch is used on the Network Setup form and on various screens in the 800 Sign on/Change Options sequence.

If True (SetToolDisplay), marks a field as being owned by a tool.
If False (NoToolDisplay), marks a field as not being owned by a tool.
If True (SetNormalDisplay), turns on a window's normal flag. Windows with their normal flag turned off are not found or deleted by fdo$Pop.

Used in Activate Flashsession Now form to disable those accounts where there is no flashsession action.

If True (SetGMenuFlags), global menu flags that are specified in the <switch_parameter> parameter of the fdo$Switch command are enabled. If False, these menu items are dimmed. Menu item flags are in the range of 101 to 228. (kMMFlagTrueStart-kMMFlagFalseEnd). IDs for global menu flags are as follows: * Global menu flag 1 = True, causes menu items with a menu flag value of 101 (kMMFlagTrueStart) to be enabled. * Global menu flag 1 = False, dims menu items with a menu flag value of 101. * Global menu flag 2 = True, causes menu items with a menu flag value of 102 (kMMFlagTrueStart+1) to be enabled. * Global menu flag 2 = False, dims menu items with a menu flag value of 102.

If True (SetTKInfoFlag), turns on the window's specified TKInfo flag. Note that these switches are only useful for tool-based forms. A tool can also set these tkInfo flags via a cbSetWindowFlag call. (See the America Online Macintosh Client Manual for more information about the cbSetWindowFlag.)
Sets the window's instance to -1 (no instance). Instance values not equal to -1 are used for windows created by tools.
Sets the window's type to a mail window type. Menu items with menu flag value 14 are enabled if the front-most window is a mail window.

rows.col1: SetWarnDisplay / NoWarnDisplay: If True, causes the warning 'This window contains unsaved data. Do you want to close it?' to appear when the member closes the window.
Note: This flag is not very useful, since it does not check to see if the window has been changed and not saved.

SetWarnTchDisplay / NoWarnTchDisplay: If True, causes the warning 'This window contains unsaved data. Do you want to close it?' to appear when the member attempts to close the window after the window has been changed or touched.
SetOnlineOnlyDisplay / NoOnlineOnlyDisplay: If True, causes the window to be closed when the member goes from online to offline or vice versa.
SetMBOnlyDisplay / NoMBOnlyDisplay: If True, causes the window to be closed if the mailbox file is closed.
SuppressAborts / NoSuppressAborts: If True, aborts (COMMAND PERIOD) are ignored.
SetMarkSendDisplay / NoMarkSendDisplay: If True, sets the Mark send flag for the window, which causes the client to send mark numbers to the host instead of field numbers in Send_Form dispatch.
SetResizeSwitches: Sets various window resizing options, depending on the value of the ID. Available ID values are as follows:
ID 1 - Deletes the window's maximum width
ID 2 - Deletes the window's maximum height
ID 3 - Causes the window to be drawn at its remembered (or default) size immediately
ID 4 - Zeros out a window's remember size ID which defeats Remember window size

40 Sends srMop start/restart message to tool whose cluster is named in subparm2 and subparm3 of <MOP_info>.

reference_tables.notes:
Example: In the example shown in Figure E.2 below, a form is created with a button field as field 1 and a list field as field 2. The button has a MOP code of 10, with a 'S1' token, a value of $04 for subparm1, a value of $02 for subparam2, and a value of $00 for subparam3. In this example, the dispatch sends all of the text of the currently selected list item to the host. When the button associated with this dispatch is clicked, the rest of the contents of the form is also sent.

This example of the send_form dispatch parameters for a button field that also has a list field is shown in Figure E.2:
<MOP_code> (<token><s_codp) <MOP_info>
Subparm!1| Subparm2 | Subparm3
Figure E.2 — Send_Form Example

rows.col3: Sends the three subparameters of the clicked button to the host.

Sends all or part of the text from the selected list item to the host. Subparm2 and subparm3 of <MOP_info> determine what part of the selected list item's text is sent. Subparm? identifies the starting offset. A value of 2 sends text starting with first character of the list item, a value of 3 sends text starting with the second character, etc. A value of 0 for subparm3 causes all of the text for the currently selected list item to be sent; otherwise the value of subparm3 is the number of characters to send.
Sends the subparameters of <MOP_info> from the dispatch of the currently selected item.

Sends a number that indicates the index of the selected list item. The number sent is the list field's field number plus the index of the selected item. For example, if the list is field 6, and the third item is selected, a value of 9 (6+3) is sent for the list field.

code_samples.explanation:
This example adds a single entry to a newly created menu.
The first command creates a new menu titled Mail positioned to the immediate left of the Windows menu. For more information, see the fdo$MakeMenu definition.
The second command adds a single entry to the new menu.

Parameter Breakdown for fdo$Menultem:
- $1025: Sets the cluster and ID for the menu item to the Mail cluster.
- 0050: Sets the menu ID to 50.
- 293 (Note: Text lists '293' in breakdown, code shows '255'): Places the new item at the end of the existing menu commands.
- 00: Specifies that this menu item is always enabled.
- $4D: Sets the command key associated with this item to "M."
- 00: Specifies that this item does not have an attached sub-menu.
- $2F $0000 $00: Assigns a dispatch to this menu item. When this item is selected from the Mail menu, the local database is asked to display the form identified by the last three bytes of the dispatch ($00, $04, $D3).
- $04 $D3: (Part of dispatch identifier)
- "Compose Mail": Sets the name of this menu item to "Compose Mail."
code_samples.sample_title: Example: Adding a Menu Entry to FDO88
